theory_query: Build a theory of how language model agents can best be augmented with memory to perform well at text games, based on the following results.

theory_statement_law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
theory_statement_law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.
theory_statement_law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
theory_statement_law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
theory_statement_law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically shows that a four-agent, tree-of-thoughts pipeline (summarization → area selection → action selection → validation) yields higher win rates and lower rule-violation error than direct or CoT queries on the evaluated non-cooperative game (Goedendag) across tested board sizes.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It documents the empirical regularity that structuring prompts into task-specific agents and selecting a bounded area of the state-space produces measurable performance and error-rate improvements in this game domain.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Within the scope of the experiments, the effect (task-decomposed agents + selective area → improved win rate and lower error) is an explicit, established empirical finding of the paper.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper collects and documents recurring empirical phenomena reported across LLMGA studies: context overload from naive concatenation, short-term temporal inconsistency (e.g., PokéLLMon switch behaviour), degradation when working memory exceeds effective capacity, and benefit from compression/summary and selective retrieval. These behaviors are illustrated with examples and referenced empirical results from prior work.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The survey synthesizes and names these failure modes and regularities across multiple papers and environments, presenting them as central design challenges for LLMGAs rather than isolated observations.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: No genuinely new empirical phenomenon is reported; the contribution is a consolidated, cross-paper articulation of known failure modes rather than discovery of previously unreported effects.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that an LLM agent using an integrated semantic knowledge graph plus linked episodic memory (AriGraph) attains substantially better task performance than agents using unstructured full history, summarization, RAG, Reflexion, or Simulacra across multiple text-game environments and competitive results on multi-hop QA.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: AriGraph shows the empirical regularity that explicitly combining semantic graph structure with episodic vertices and a two-stage retrieval (semantic → episodic) produces consistent, large gains in partially observable, long-horizon text environments and improves retrieval for multi-hop QA.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The empirical effect — that a hybrid structured+episodic graph memory with selective graph-based retrieval materially outperforms common unstructured or single-modality memory schemes in these benchmarks — is presented and validated as a central contribution.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work (KG-A2C, GATA, LIGHT agents) shows structured external memory (knowledge graphs) and prompt-like conditioning improve performance in text games. The paper empirically demonstrates that a single model can be trained to produce distinct per-character behaviors when given learned soft character prompts and per-character action-logit outputs. It also shows that a frozen core plus an attention module can compose pre-trained per-character logits to enable fast few-shot learning of a blended new character.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper introduces the empirical phenomenon that (1) learning soft character prompts together with per-character logit heads allows one model to emulate multiple distinct characters without behavioral entanglement, and (2) an attention-based fusion over raw action logits enables few-shot creation of a new blended character while preserving prior behaviors.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: These specific empirical outcomes (prompt-conditioned per-character logits preventing entanglement and logits-level attention enabling efficient few-shot blending) are contributions of this paper and are presented with experimental evidence in LIGHT environments.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that injecting affordance knowledge (sourced from ConceptNet and integrated as KG triples or via pretraining) improves action selection and cumulative reward across many ScienceWorld tasks, and that preserving a memory of previous correct actions (MCA) yields smaller, task-dependent gains. It also shows that naïve concatenation of affordance strings or adding affordances as raw textual input often harms performance (information overload).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory emphasizes a general phenomenon: that memory augmentation benefits depend on memory structure, selective access, and composition rather than mere volume or modality, and that unstructured brute-force context retention fails to scale in complex text-game agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The core empirical phenomenon (structured/selective memory matters more than raw/contextual volume; KG-based affordances outperform plain concatenation; MCA helps but less consistently) is explicitly established by the paper's experiments and ablations.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that a dynamically constructed knowledge graph plus a template-based action space (with a graph-constrained decoder and valid-action supervision) improves learning speed and final performance across a broad suite of 28 interactive-fiction games; ablations show graph masking/attention speed convergence and that unconstrained, word-level decoding performs poorly. These results establish that structure and selectivity in state and action representations materially affect agent performance in large natural-language action spaces.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory-level phenomenon asserts more broadly that robust performance in LLM-based text-game agents requires a hybrid of structured (relational) and episodic/reflection memory modalities and principled selective retrieval/composition, not merely larger memory capacity or unstructured history retention.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Relative to this paper, the novel empirical claim is the explicit requirement for integration of structured relational memory with episodic/reflective memory and selective composition; the paper documents the importance of structured representations and selectivity but does not provide evidence for the necessity or benefit of an episodic/reflection memory modality.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that autonomously generated structured augmentations (attribute mining, turn/session granularity, priority ordering) combined with selective retrieval (attribute-filtering or embedding-based search) improves downstream performance (QA, conversational recommendation, event summarization) and retrieval recall relative to baselines that use all history or unstructured memory.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: MemInsight explicitly documents the empirical effect that structured, attribute-centric memory augmentations plus selective retrieval reduce noise/volume of retrieved context and yield consistent gains across multiple dialogue tasks and backbone models.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The core phenomenon — that memory structure and selective retrieval materially affect LLM-agent performance in multi-session dialogue tasks — is directly established and evaluated in the paper.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that learned, dynamic graph-structured belief states (GATA) improve policy learning and generalization across a distribution of TextWorld games relative to text-only baselines, and that access to full ground-truth graphs yields a higher performance upper bound. It also reports that discrete updater variants can be brittle and that continuous belief graphs encode actionable relational information for decision making.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The evaluated theory claims a broader phenomenon: robust performance in LLM-based text-game agents requires a principled hybrid of structured (relational/graph) memory and episodic/reflection-style memory, with structure, selectivity, and retrieval quality as important as raw memory volume or modality.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper establishes the empirical phenomenon that structured graph memory helps in TextWorld, but it does not establish the stronger, domain-general phenomenon that a hybrid structured+episodic memory is a fundamental, necessary requirement for robust performance across LLM agents and long-horizon partially observable tasks.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that augmenting LLM-driven agents with a persistent natural-language memory stream plus selective retrieval, periodic reflection (recursive synthesis), and planning produces markedly more believable individual behavior and emergent social dynamics (information diffusion, relationship formation, coordination) than ablated architectures. Ablation experiments quantify degraded believability when observation, reflection, or planning are removed. The end-to-end two-day multi-agent simulation shows these components produce concrete emergent phenomena in the Smallville sandbox.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory frames a named, broader phenomenon: that robust performance in LLM-based text-game agents requires a principled integration of structured (relational/graph-like) memory and episodic/reflection memory, with selective retrieval and composition being critical beyond mere memory volume or naive context concatenation.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The core empirical phenomenon the theory asserts—selective, structured retrieval combined with episodic/reflection leading to improved agent behavior—is directly demonstrated and measured in the paper’s sandbox and ablation studies, so the phenomenon is present and empirically supported within the paper's domain.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically shows that injecting symbolic-module actions (arithmetic, navigation, sorting, KB lookup) into the action space yields large, consistent performance and efficiency gains for a behavior-cloned transformer across four text-game benchmarks, raising average task performance to near-ceiling while leaving the tested RL baseline (DRRN) unimproved. The experiments used parametric train/dev/test splits and report aggregated results over 100 unseen games per benchmark. The paper also documents the action-space expansion caused by some modules (e.g., ~530 KB queries).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It introduces and names the practical phenomenon that treating external symbolic tools as first-class actions (action-space injection) enables a sequence-model (behavior cloning with a pretrained transformer) to reliably exploit those tools and substantially improve multi-step interactive task performance and efficiency.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This empirical effect is explicitly demonstrated and established by the paper for the tested domains and agent setup, but it does not assert or test any claims about episodic or reflection memory or the need to integrate structured and episodic memory modalities.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that adding a vector-based Memory Retriever (embedding store + Faiss top-k retrieval) and complementary modules (Self-Refinement, Self-Verification) improves agents' factual and inferential accuracy, increases similarity between chat histories and full scripts, and raises murderer-identification accuracy and civilian win rate in the Jubensha multi-agent text-game setting.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a broader phenomenon: that not merely memory volume or modality but the internal structure of memory, selective retrieval strategies, and how retrieved content is composed with ongoing reasoning are critical determinants of agent performance and scalability; naive unstructured concatenation leads to overload or sparsity and fails on complex, long-horizon tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already documents the empirical effect that semantic retrieval plus reflection/verification improves performance in this domain, but it does not empirically or conceptually establish the stronger, domain-general phenomenon that structured (e.g., graph) + episodic hybrid memory and principled composition are a fundamental requirement across tasks.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent based on Theory of Mind in Guandan (搜蛋): A Multi-Player Cooperative Game under Imperfect Information

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically documents that off-the-shelf LLM agents underperform a SOTA RL agent (Danzero+) in the multi-player imperfect-information card game Guandan and that when presented with very long valid-action lists LLMs strongly prefer the first few listed actions. It also shows that inserting an RL-based action recommender (top-k filtering) and ToM-guided planning raises performance across multiple LLMs and languages.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: An explicitly observed failure mode: action-list overload combined with a front-of-list selection bias in LLM decision behavior in a realistic multi-agent game, and the empirical finding that an RL-based top-k action recommender plus ToM planning mitigates this failure mode.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides concrete, measured evidence of the action-list overload/front-bias in a complex cooperative imperfect-information setting and demonstrates a reproducible mitigation (top-k recommender + ToM) that improves outcomes across model families and language settings.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work (RAG, ReAct, Reflexion, ExpeL and related retrieval/reflection methods) established that retrieval or episodic reflection can improve LLM outputs and can help per-task adaptation in text-only settings. Empirical evidence for large, consistent gains specifically in sequential decision-making across both text games and embodied multimodal benchmarks was limited in the literature.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: This paper empirically shows that contextually keyed retrieval of compact episodic traces (task/plan/trajectory windows) substantially improves success rates and rewards across four diverse benchmarks (ALFWorld, WebShop, Franka Kitchen, Meta-World) and across different LLM/VLM backbones.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The concrete cross-benchmark empirical effect—that selective, modality-aware episodic retrieval plus windowed composition into in-context examples yields consistent, large improvements for both text and embodied multimodal agents—is demonstrated and quantified here.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that a zero-shot prompted LLM augmented with external symbolic modules attains high performance on several symbolic text-game tasks and reports task-specific memory-related failure modes (e.g., forgetting navigation routes, limited capacity affecting sorting). It does not formulate a general, named phenomenon linking memory organization to scaling or cross-task generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a general phenomenon: memory augmentation benefits for LLM agents depend critically on memory structure, selective retrieval, and composition (structured relational stores + episodic/reflection), not merely on memory volume or modality.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper contains empirical hints about memory limits and failure modes but does not explicitly identify, name, or systematically characterize the proposed general phenomenon that structured/selective memory (vs. brute-force context) is the decisive factor for scaling and generalization.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates an empirical phenomenon: replacing full-text observation histories with Unix-style text deltas (diff history) yields large improvements in sample efficiency and final performance for LM agents in procedurally-generated text environments (strong effect in NetHack, efficiency gains in BabyAI-Text). It also documents environment-dependent behavior (token compression in high-dimensional NetHack, token inflation in BabyAI-Text).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory-level phenomenon asserts a broader, domain-general regularity: robust performance and generalization of LLM agents in complex, partially-observable, long-horizon tasks require not only memory volume but a principled hybrid of structured memory and episodic/reflection memory with selective retrieval/composition.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper establishes that a particular form of structured memory (diff deltas) materially improves agent behavior, but it does not empirically establish the stronger, domain-general phenomenon that a hybrid structured+episodic memory is a fundamental requirement across LLM-agent domains.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically shows that assembling multiple frozen, role-aligned policy experts plus a single trainable ("hot") expert with an attention module and a knowledge-graph (KG) state representation yields improved zero-shot and few-shot transfer and greater sample efficiency in their LIGHT-based open-ended text-roleplaying tasks. It also reports failure modes: degraded performance when relevant experts are absent and sensitivity to many irrelevant (distractor) experts. These effects are measured and reported across seeds and experimental conditions.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement asserts a broader phenomenon: that memory augmentation benefits depend more on memory structure, selective access, and composition than on brute memory volume or modality, i.e., structured/selective memory is as critical as memory size or modality for robust agent performance.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides a concrete instance consistent with the theory (structured KG + selective attention over experts helps transfer), but it does not establish the general phenomenon that structure/selectivity universally trumps or equates to memory size/modality across agents, tasks, or long-horizon settings.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that pretrained autoregressive LMs (GPT2-medium) fine-tuned with a small set of demonstrations and augmented via iterated action modeling (IAM) achieve large improvements in ALFWorld; IAM can recover much of the performance of having many more demonstrations. The implementation relies on concatenating goal/observations/actions into a sliding context window (max sequence length 1000 tokens) during training and evaluation.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory claims a distinct empirical phenomenon: robust, scalable performance in complex, partially-observable, long-horizon text games requires a hybrid of structured (relational/graph/attribute) and episodic/reflection memory and selective retrieval/composition — not merely greater memory volume or naive concatenation.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The ALFWorld paper does not identify or name this phenomenon (the necessity of hybrid structured–episodic memory or a systematic failure mode of unstructured concatenation) and provides no experiments or measurements that establish it.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper establishes an empirical phenomenon: storing compact, verbal self-reflections (short natural-language summaries of failed trials) in a small long-term memory yields substantial, consistent performance improvements for LLM agents across decision-making (ALFWorld), multi-hop QA (HotPotQA), and code generation (HumanEval/MBPP/Leetcode) compared to baselines and to na"ive episodic trajectory inclusion.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a broader phenomenon: robust performance and generalization in LLM-based text-game agents fundamentally require a hybrid of structured (graphs/attributes) and episodic/reflection-based memory, with selectivity and retrieval/composition being critical.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The Reflexion paper documents the benefit of distilled verbal episodic memory but does not identify, test, or demonstrate the specific phenomenon that a hybrid structured+episodic memory is necessary; that specific phenomenon is not present in the paper.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically shows that augmenting LLM agents with positive-experience reflection plus a managed memory (dual short-term/long-term buffers that store reflection tuples) improves success rates on the ScienceWorld benchmark relative to baselines (ReAct, Reflexion, CALM), with larger relative gains for smaller models and a performance drop when sampling only from failures. It documents the behavioral regularity that including successful experiences sustains momentum and reduces 'tilt' in medium-difficulty tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory claims a broader phenomenon: robust performance and generalization in LLM-based text-game agents require an integrated hybrid of structured (graph/attribute/summary) and episodic/reflection memory rather than merely more or persistent unstructured memory.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper demonstrates an episodic/managed-memory phenomenon (benefit of positive reflections) but does not identify or empirically demonstrate the specific phenomenon that hybridizing structured relational memory with episodic reflection is necessary for robustness or generalization.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates empirically that structured memory in the form of knowledge graphs improves state modeling and valid-action prediction in partially observable text games, and that predicting graph differences (G_{t+1}-G_t) yields a much smaller, more tractable target than predicting full next-state graphs. It shows consistent performance gains across multiple unseen test games and ablations validating these effects within the text-game domain.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory claims a broader phenomenon: that robust performance and generalization of LLM-based agents in complex, partially observable, long-horizon domains require a principled hybrid of structured (relational) memory and episodic/reflective memory, plus selective retrieval and composition mechanisms.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The necessity of integrating structured relational memory with episodic/reflective memory (and the claim that structure/selectivity/retrieval are as important as mere memory size or modality) is not empirically or conceptually established in the paper and therefore represents a new phenomenon relative to this work.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work on text-based games used learned textual encoders (often trained from scratch), object-centric retrieval, and explicit knowledge-graph state representations; some works used LMs for action generation but not as a frozen/fine-tuned encoder feeding value networks. Empirically, agents trained end-to-end from scratch suffer sample inefficiency and memorization in Jericho benchmarks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: This paper demonstrates that inserting a compact pre-trained transformer (DistilBERT), fine-tuned on out-of-evaluation human game transcripts and used as the encoder for DRRN/TDQN, measurably improves sample efficiency, stability, and final scores across a suite of 14 IF games and attains new SOTA on several games.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The concrete empirical effect — that a single compact pre-trained LM, fine-tuned on general gameplay transcripts and reused (without per-game LM fine-tuning) as the language encoder, can substitute for more complex/structured components and improve RL learning in Jericho games — is not previously established in the cited literature and is presented as a central empirical finding.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work has shown LLMs can serve as commonsense priors for planning (e.g., LLM-as-policy/LLM-MCTS) and that separate memory or structured-state representations can aid text-game agents; vanilla LLM agents and naive MCTS/PUCT struggle on long-horizon, sparse-reward text games with bottlenecks. Empirical benefits of reflective/self-improving LLMs have been demonstrated in interactive settings, but mostly outside of MCTS-integration at scale.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: This paper demonstrates the empirical phenomenon that conditioning an LLM prior inside PUCT on a short in-trial trajectory window plus a bounded set of cross-trial episodic 'reflections' materially improves initial-planning performance across nine Jericho games and helps the search avoid bottleneck failure modes. Ablations show removing cross-trial or in-trial memory substantially degrades scores, indicating the memory modalities are causally related to the observed gains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The specific empirical regularity—that selective, bounded cross-trial reflections combined with short in-trial context change the LLM action prior during MCTS simulations in a way that yields large, consistent initial-planning gains on challenging text-game benchmarks—is not previously documented.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that a pretrained language-model-based action generator (CALM) combined with an RL re-ranker (DRRN) yields higher average normalized scores on a suite of 28 Jericho text games than prior handicap-free baselines, and that generation hyperparameters (top-k) trade off precision vs. recall of admissible and gold actions. It also shows that pretraining on WebText plus fine-tuning on human transcripts materially improves the quality of generated, parseable actions.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory asserts a distinct empirical phenomenon: robust generalization and scaling in LLM-based text-game agents fundamentally require a hybrid memory architecture that integrates structured relational stores (e.g., knowledge/ belief graphs) with episodic/reflection memory and selective retrieval/composition.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper documents the practical benefit of selective candidate generation and the k-dependent precision/recall tradeoff, but it does not identify or empirically demonstrate the novel phenomenon that a principled hybrid of structured relational memory and episodic/reflection memory is a necessary condition for robust scaling/generalization; that necessity is not observed in the paper.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides an explicit implementation-level mechanism: a graph G = (V_s,E_s,V_e,E_e) where semantic triplets form the KG, episodic vertices store raw observations, episodic edges bind triplets to observations, and retrieval proceeds by embedding-based semantic search followed by episodic ranking using a defined relevance score.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It attributes the performance gains to this causal structure: compact relational representations in the semantic graph support navigation and planning while episodic bindings supply rich context for procedural recall; the two-stage retrieval composes these modalities for decision-time reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The concrete mechanistic account — episodic edges that explicitly bind semantic triplets to observations plus the presented semantic-then-episodic retrieval algorithms and scoring heuristics — is an explicit, implemented explanation for why hybrid memory improves agent behavior in the reported settings.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Previous methods described components separately: retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for knowledge grounding, ReAct for interleaving reasoning and action, and reflection-style methods that pass episodes back to LLMs for improvement. However, a single operational mechanism tying retrieval-key generation, adaptive multi-component similarity scoring, and selective windowing into the agent loop was not firmly established.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: RAP specifies an explicit pipeline (Memory → Reasoner → Retriever → Executor) with retrieval-key generation from action plans, a weighted similarity score combining task/plan/key components, modality-specific encoders (sentence-transformers/CLIP), adaptive component weights, and passage of centered trajectory windows to the Executor for in-context learning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides an operational mechanistic account that explains how structured episodic retrieval (with retrieval keys and weighted similarity) integrates into the agent loop to improve planning; the particular combination and implementation details are provided and evaluated.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent based on Theory of Mind in Guandan (搜蛋): A Multi-Player Cooperative Game under Imperfect Information

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers a causal account for its empirical results: LLMs struggle to process lengthy unstructured action/context lists, which reduces consideration of later options, and explicit belief inference (first/second-order ToM prompts) supplies structured information that aids planning and evaluation.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: A pragmatic mechanistic explanation tying selective candidate filtering (top-k action recommender) to reduced input-processing burden and showing that explicit belief summaries appended to planning prompts improve action evaluation.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This is an empirical, scoped mechanistic account (input overload → candidate filtering necessary; belief summaries → better evaluations) but it is limited to the demonstrated pipeline and lacks a formal theory of memory or retrieval; it is presented as an operational explanation rather than a new underlying cognitive/mechanistic law.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers an explanatory account that gains arise because the LM supplies linguistic priors, world knowledge, and a game-sense (affordances) which improve observation encoding, reward-cue extraction, and action-value estimation; prior work has speculated about or used auxiliary modules (KGs, retrieval) to address similar problems but did not provide the same LM-centered causal story empirically tested here.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The authors attribute improved decision-making and some exploration benefits to LM-derived priors that highlight salient objects/actions (e.g., lantern, egg) even when in-game reward signals are sparse, enabling the agent to choose higher-value actions earlier.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The causal explanation is offered explicitly in the paper but is qualitative and not mechanistically analyzed (no causal ablation isolating world-knowledge vs. linguistic encoding vs. transcript fine-tuning in a mechanistic framework).

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper specifies concrete mechanisms: a natural-language memory stream of observations with timestamps and access metadata, a retrieval scorer that combines recency (decay), LM-assigned importance, and embedding-based relevance, a reflection process that synthesizes higher-level insights (reflection trees), and top-down recursive planning decomposed into actions. It also uses an environment tree to ground actions to locations.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory advances a mechanistic causal claim that structured relational representations (graphs/attributes) supply a compact scaffold for navigation and relational reasoning while episodic/reflection memory supplies dynamic adaptation and procedural recall, and that their principled integration produces synergy beyond either modality alone.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper implements and causally evaluates many of the operational mechanisms (retrieval scoring, reflection, planning) and includes an environment tree for grounding, but it does not formalize or experimentally isolate the specific, general-purpose synergy claim that an explicit relational knowledge-graph modality combined with episodic/reflection memory is necessary across domains; that stronger mechanistic claim is presented by the theory more than empirically established by the paper.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper describes mechanistic components used in prior systems (memory consolidation triggers, memory structuring: chunks/kv/trees/graphs/parametric, metadata and semantic retrieval, active maintenance, and reflective loops) and explains how these mechanisms are intended to affect retrieval relevance and decision consistency.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It assembles these mechanisms into causal narratives (e.g., structured storage + selective retrieval → more relevant context for working memory → improved planning/consistency), but stops short of a formal, novel causal theorem asserting necessity of a specific hybrid memory architecture.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: An integrated causal claim that hybrid structured (graph/tree/kv) and episodic/reflection memory together are required for robust, generalizable LLMGA performance is inferable from the synthesis but is not explicitly formalized or proven in the paper.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers applied mechanistic explanations: affordances narrow the action search space and focus learning on relevant interactions; KGs provide a structured state representation that reduces interference; MCA reinforces successful strategies and reduces repetition of failed actions; concatenation can introduce interference or overload.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory advances a richer mechanistic account proposing complementary causal roles: structured memory supplies compact relational representations for navigation/reasoning, episodic/reflection memory supplies dynamic adaptation and procedural recall, and selective retrieval/composition between these modalities balances compression, relevance, and reasoning depth.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides partial causal explanations at the architectural/empirical level but does not articulate the fuller, general causal architecture of hybrid structured↔episodic interaction and selective-retrieval dynamics posited by the theory; that integrated mechanistic story is an extrapolation beyond the paper's analyses.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper describes concrete operational mechanisms (embedding-based retrieval, decomposition-based self-refinement, and fact-checking self-verification) and shows that adding these modules causally improves downstream metrics in their experiments.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory offers a mechanistic explanation for why a hybrid of structured relational memory and episodic/reflection memory would be synergistic: structured stores give compact relational representations for navigation/reasoning, while episodic/reflection enables dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from interactions; selective retrieval/composition manages compression vs. relevance trade-offs.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper implements and evidences reflective and retrieval mechanisms but does not introduce or evaluate an explicit structured-relational memory (graphs/attributes) nor formalize the proposed trade-offs between compression, retrieval relevance, and reasoning depth; the proposed mechanistic synergy is therefore not established by the paper.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers mechanistic explanations for why diff history helps: (1) task-agnostic relational abstraction localizes salient changes and provides a denser learning signal, and (2) token-level compression extends effective context horizon in high-dimensional settings, both improving representation learning and sample efficiency.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a causal mechanism at a higher level: structured memory supplies compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, episodic/reflection memory supports dynamic adaptation and procedural recall, and selective retrieval/composition between them is necessary to balance compression, relevance, and deep reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper supplies concrete, local mechanisms for a single structured representation (diffs), but it does not test or instantiate the multi-component causal architecture (persistent structured store + episodic/reflection module + selective retrieval) asserted by the theory; that multi-part mechanistic claim remains an extrapolation.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Mechanistic elements are known separately: PUCT uses a prior policy to bias exploration, LLMs can provide priors/predictions, and reflection methods can produce episodic summaries; prior works have not operationalized these three pieces as a single online mechanism inside MCTS for text-game planning.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper proposes a concrete mechanism: compute an LLM action-probability prior conditioned on (i) in-trial memory (a short trajectory window) and (ii) cross-trial reflections produced when simulated trajectories fail, then plug that dynamic prior into the PUCT exploration term to steer simulation distribution and avoid repeatedly exploring known-failure actions.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That mechanism—closed-loop, online updating of the LLM prior inside PUCT driven by episodic reflections generated during the search and used within subsequent simulations—is an explicit operational contribution that combines existing pieces in a way not previously implemented or evaluated on long-horizon text games; however, it builds on and composes previously published ideas rather than introducing an entirely new causal variable.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper specifies mechanistic components: a persistent structured state (knowledge graph) embedded by graph attention, frozen expert modules producing action logits and critic values, value-seeding (multiplying action distributions by critic-predicted returns), and an attention module that projects observations and expert logits to compute mixing weights; it also describes exploration mitigations (epsilon-greedy, auxiliary loss).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a mechanistic causal account that robust LLM-agent performance arises from the synergy between structured relational memory (e.g., graphs) and episodic/reflective memory plus selective retrieval and composition during reasoning and planning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper implements structured memory (KG) and selective routing (attention/value-seeding) and therefore supplies concrete mechanisms that map onto parts of the theory, it does not implement or analyze an explicit episodic/reflective memory subsystem nor demonstrates the claimed synergistic causal role of a structured+episodic hybrid across tasks.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides a mechanistic account specific to its method: (1) summarization produces a compact, consistent representation; (2) area selection bounds the state set to avoid exponential memory growth; (3) validation reduces illegal actions; and (4) multi-agent separation reduces per-agent memory/cognitive load.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory asserts a different mechanistic claim: that robust LLM-agent performance requires an explicit hybrid of structured (relational/graph-like) memory plus episodic/reflection memory together with selective retrieval/composition during reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper implements decomposition and selective retrieval but does not articulate, operationalize, or evaluate a hybrid structured-versus-episodic memory mechanism (graphs + episodic/reflection) as the causal explanation; that hybrid mechanistic claim is not present or tested in the paper.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper describes concrete mechanisms: a KG-A2C backbone (structured knowledge graph), recurrent embedding of observations (local/episodic context), learned soft character prompts, stacked per-character action-logit heads, and a feed-forward attention module that weights raw logits to produce a fused action distribution for a new character.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The broader theory claims a causal mechanism that robust LLM-agent performance requires a principled hybrid of structured (relational/graph) memory and episodic/reflection memory combined with selective retrieval and composition interleaved with reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper implements and demonstrates particular engineering mechanisms (KG + prompts + logit-attention) but does not articulate or experimentally validate the general causal claim that a structured–episodic hybrid plus selective retrieval is a necessary, general mechanism for robust scaling and generalization of LLM-based agents.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers mechanistic, engineering-level explanations for its results: pretrained linguistic priors make action text generation grammatical, action modeling trains policies to imitate demonstrations, and iterated action modeling augments the training set by adding successful trajectories while retaining the language/action modeling head.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a specific causal mechanism absent from the paper: complementary roles for a compact structured relational store (for navigation/reasoning) and an episodic/reflection store (for adaptation, procedural recall, learning from failures), together with selective retrieval/composition mechanisms that trade off compression, relevance, and reasoning depth.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Those specific causal components and their claimed synergistic interactions are not presented, analyzed, or tested in the paper.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper gives concrete mechanistic accounts for why knowledge graphs and template actions help: the KG provides a compact relational state embedding (via GATs) used by the policy/critic and a graph-derived mask that constrains object decoding, and template actions reduce combinatorial generation complexity; supervised valid-action losses further shape decoding towards actionable commands.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a new mechanistic synergy: structured memory supplies compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning while episodic/reflection memory supplies dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from failure; crucially, selective retrieval and composition across these modalities are claimed necessary to balance compression, relevance, and deep multi-step reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper contains mechanisms for structured relational memory and selective decoding but does not instantiate or analyze any episodic/reflection memory modality or their interaction; therefore the causal claim that hybrid structured+episodic memory and selective composition are required is not present in the paper.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides a mechanistic account for the observed gains: an Actor–Evaluator–Self-Reflection loop where sparse scalar feedback is amplified into actionable natural-language reflections that become long-term in-context memory, improving credit assignment and guiding subsequent in-context generation without weight updates.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory attributes performance to complementary causal roles of two memory modalities: structured relational stores (supporting compact relational reasoning and traversal) and episodic/reflection traces (supporting procedural recall and trial-to-trial adaptation), plus selective retrieval/composition between them.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Reflexion explains how distilled verbal episodic memory functions but does not posit or evaluate explicit structured-memory mechanisms or their causal interaction with episodic reflections; the hybrid causal mechanism and the claim that selective composition across modalities is the critical causal factor are not evaluated in the paper.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides a mechanistic account for Sweet&Sour: agents verbalize positive and negative experiences, store reflections as tuples in a managed short-term/long-term memory, and retrieve those reflections as additional context to guide subsequent decisions, which the authors argue supplies richer context and sustains successful policies.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a different mechanistic explanation: a synergy where structured memory provides compact relational/belief representations for navigation and multi-step reasoning while episodic/reflection memory supplies dynamic procedural recall and adaptation, and selective retrieval/composition across these modalities balances compression, relevance, and depth of reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The hybrid mechanistic account (causal role of graph/attribute-structured memory composed with episodic reflections and selective retrieval strategies) is not developed or tested in the paper, which only operationalizes and reasons about episodic/managed reflections.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides an operational mechanistic account: attribute mining (entity- vs conversation-centric), granularity choices (turn vs session), attribute prioritization, and two retrieval modes (comprehensive vs refined; attribute-filter vs embedding similarity) that together cause more precise retrieval and improved downstream reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory posits a distinct causal mechanism: a required synergy between structured relational memory (e.g., graphs/attributes) and episodic/reflection-style memory (dynamic procedural recall and reflection) whose selective composition during retrieval is necessary for robustness and generalization in complex agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While MemInsight gives concrete mechanisms for structured augmentation and selective retrieval, it does not present, analyze, or empirically evaluate a hybrid structured–episodic causal architecture or the specific interaction dynamics the theory claims; that hybrid mechanistic claim is not provided by this paper.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides mechanistic explanations for its empirical gains: casting KG prediction as graph-difference prediction reduces output length and difficulty; multi-task training couples action and state prediction beneficially; and an SOS (Set-of-Sequences) loss enforces permutation invariance improving decoder training.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory advances a different mechanistic account centered on synergy between compact relational (structured) memory and episodic/reflection memory that enables dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from failures, and emphasizes selective retrieval/composition (semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, graph traversal) as crucial mechanisms.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Those specific mechanisms (episodic/reflective memory implementations, selective retrieval and composition policies, and their synergistic causal role with structured KGs) are not implemented or evidenced in the paper, so the mechanistic claims exceed the paper's contributions.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides mechanistic components: a recurrent graph-updater (f_delta, RNN h_t, f_d) that accumulates incremental belief updates, R-GCN graph encoding to capture multi-relational state, attention-based aggregation for selective access, and self-supervised pretraining (OG and COC) to make belief graphs encode relevant semantics.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory advances a specific causal mechanism: the synergy between structured relational memory (for compact navigation/reasoning) and episodic/reflection memory (for dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from failures), and the necessity of selective retrieval/composition between these modalities to scale and generalize.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper explicates mechanisms for learned structured memory and uses an RNN updater (which provides a form of temporal memory), it does not articulate or test the explicit causal synergy or necessity of combining structured memory with a distinct episodic/reflection module and selective retrieval policies as the primary mechanism for robust, scalable LLM agents.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explains improved agent behavior primarily via prompting design and integration of external symbolic modules (calculation, navigation, sorting, knowledge lookup) and notes that constrained prompts reduce errors; it does not provide a causal model of memory architectures or retrieval policies.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a specific mechanistic account: a hybrid memory architecture—structured relational stores (graphs/attributes) plus episodic/reflection memory—with selective retrieval/composition produces synergy enabling compact relational reasoning and dynamic adaptation.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That concrete causal/mechanistic hypothesis (hybrid structured + episodic memory, selective retrieval as the mediating mechanism enabling scalability and generalization) is not offered, analyzed, or tested in the paper and therefore represents a genuinely new explanatory proposal relative to the paper's content.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers mechanistic explanations tied to the experimental setup: behavior cloning succeeds because gold trajectories teach the model when and how to call modules and pretrained transformers encode useful priors, while the DRRN struggles due to myopic reward signals, exploration difficulty in large action spaces, and ungeneralizable training signals. The paper links performance differences to training data (gold trajectories) and action-space properties.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory proposes a distinct mechanistic account that robust LLM-agent behavior requires a principled synergy between structured memory (e.g., graphs, attributes, summaries) and episodic/reflection memory with selective retrieval and composition to balance compression, relevance, and deep reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The hybrid structured+episodic memory mechanism, selective retrieval policies, and their causal role in scaling/generalization are not proposed, analyzed, or experimentally validated in the paper; these explanatory claims are absent and cannot be reconstructed from the paper's experiments alone.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers a causal account at the algorithmic level: CALM supplies linguistic priors that reduce the effective action space, and DRRN learns Q-values over the reduced candidate set so RL can exploit promising actions; pretraining and human-transcript fine-tuning are causal factors improving generation quality and downstream performance.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a specific mechanistic explanation that performance improvements hinge on two complementary memory mechanisms—compact structured relational representations for navigation/reasoning and episodic/reflection memory for dynamic adaptation and procedural recall—plus selective retrieval and composition to balance compression and relevance.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper explains why language-model priors help by reducing action-space and improving candidate quality, it does not posit, formalize, or test the particular causal mechanism that a structured memory + episodic memory synergy (with selective retrieval) is required; that mechanistic account is new relative to the experiments and analyses presented.

unification.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The core contribution of the paper is an explicit unified reference architecture (memory, reasoning, perception–action) and a taxonomy that maps memory/reasoning designs to game genres and challenges, bringing together diverse prior systems under one framework.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It explicitly unifies many prior works by categorizing memory designs, retrieval/consolidation strategies, reasoning modalities, and interface patterns, and by presenting summary tables that map existing systems into this shared design space.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The unification is an explicit, central survey-level contribution that organizes prior results under a common conceptual framework; this is presented as a synthesis rather than a new scientific law.

unification.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior literature used unstructured retrieval stores (RAG/vector), episodic logs or summaries, or knowledge-graph methods (mainly for QA) but rarely integrated these into a single interactive world-model used for planning and action selection.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper explicitly unifies semantic (knowledge-graph) and episodic memories into a single graph-based world model, shows how retrieval, planning, and ReAct-style decision-making operate over that unified structure, and presents ablations separating components.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The explicit claim and operationalization that semantic and episodic memories should be treated as interconnected parts of a single graph memory (with bridging retrieval) and that this unification yields practical benefits is a core, documented contribution.

unification.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies multiple components—comprehensive memory storage, selective retrieval, recursive reflection, and planning—into a single agent architecture and demonstrates their joint contribution to believability via controlled ablations and a multi-agent simulation. The architecture and evaluations treat these components as an integrated system rather than isolated tricks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a higher-level unifying principle: structured memory modalities and episodic/reflection modalities are complementary and must be integrated (with selective retrieval/composition) as a single requirement for robust LLM agent performance in complex partially observable environments.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already performs this unification at the architectural and empirical level for its domain—memory+retrieval+reflection+planning are presented and evaluated as an integrated framework—so the core unification the theory claims is present and established in the paper.

unification.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: There exist multiple related strands—RAG for retrieval, ReAct for reasoning+acting, and episodic reflection/experience-based learning—but they have largely been presented and evaluated as distinct approaches or in narrow settings.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: RAP explicitly unifies retrieval-augmented generation, episodic trajectory storage, retrieval-key driven retrieval, and ReAct-style planning into a single, applied framework that is used across text-only and multimodal embodied agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The practical unification that repackages these prior ideas into one end-to-end retrieval-augmented planning loop (with modality-aware retrieval and windowed composition) is presented and empirically validated, though it builds on recognizable prior components.

unification.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: MemInsight unifies several practical components (attribute mining, annotation/prioritization, and both attribute-based and embedding-based retrieval) into one implementable memory pipeline and demonstrates their combined utility across tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a higher-level unification: that diverse prior memory approaches (structured knowledge representations, episodic/reflective memory, selective retrieval) are instances of a single fundamental requirement — a hybrid structured–episodic memory architecture for robust LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper performs a methodological unification at the pipeline level, but it does not claim or demonstrate the broader conceptual unification across structured and episodic memory paradigms as a single underlying principle; that broader unification is not present in the paper and therefore represents a novel claim relative to it.

unification.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper combines existing pieces—tree of thoughts, multi-agent task decomposition, and supervised fine-tuning from self-play—to form a practical pipeline and reports that this combination improves performance on its test game.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a unifying principle that structured memory + episodic/reflection memory with selective retrieval is the single underlying requirement explaining robustness and generalization of LLM agents in complex text-game settings.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not explicitly claim or derive such a broad unification across prior methods; while elements overlap with prior work, the specific single-principle unification (hybrid memory as the explanatory core) is not articulated or proven and would require synthesizing multiple literatures beyond this paper's scope.

unification.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly experiments with two knowledge modalities (affordances as structured KG triples and episodic memory of correct actions) and their combinations across four agent architectures, reporting when each helps and when combined injection helps or harms.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a unifying principle: structured (graph-like) and episodic (reflection-based) memory modalities are complementary and together constitute a necessary hybrid memory architecture for robust generalization in LLM-based agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Although the paper supplies the empirical building blocks and shows complementary benefits in some cases, it does not formalize or claim the general unification that these modalities are instances of a single fundamental requirement; that conceptual unification is a synthesis beyond the paper.

unification.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies two technical components—a knowledge-graph-based state representation and a template-based (graph-masked) action decoding process—and demonstrates that their combination explains improved scalability and performance in large natural-language action spaces within IF games.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory-level unification claim is broader: it asserts that multiple prior techniques (structured relational memory, episodic/reflective memory, retrieval/selectivity mechanisms) should be seen as instances of one underlying requirement for robust LLM-based agent performance in partially observable, long-horizon tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already unifies state-side structure and action-side templating; extending that unification to include episodic/reflection memory as a necessary co-equal component is not asserted or demonstrated and therefore constitutes a new synthesis beyond the paper.

unification.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Existing literature contains multiple approaches for state representation and partial observability in text games: explicit evolving knowledge graphs, retrieval/object-centric history mechanisms, template/LM-based action generation, and end-to-end learned encoders; performance comparisons exist between several of these families.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: Through empirical comparison the paper shows that LM-based priors (as an encoder) can in practice achieve comparable or better performance than some KG-based and attention/graph-based architectures on multiple benchmarks, suggesting that LM priors can serve as an alternative source of structured knowledge in these settings.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper stops short of formally unifying these approaches into a single theoretical framework; the suggested equivalence/substitution relation between LMs and explicit structured state (KGs) is an empirical takeaway that can be derived from their comparisons but is not posed or proven as a unifying principle.

unification.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper unifies several technical components into a single model (learned belief graphs, two self-supervised pretraining objectives, R-GCN encoding, attention-based fusion, and RL-based action selection) and positions this unified architecture as effective for generalization in text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a higher-level unification: that multiple prior approaches and phenomena in agent memory and reasoning can be understood as instances of a single principle (hybrid structured-episodic memory with selective retrieval and composition).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not explicitly make this broader conceptual unification across memory paradigms; although its components could be interpreted as pieces of such a unification, the paper neither frames nor defends an overarching principle that subsumes disparate memory/agent methods.

unification.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Separate literatures exist: LLM-as-prior/planner in simple planning tasks, reflection/self-improvement for LLM agents, and structured-state/knowledge-graph memory for RL agents in text games; these have been cited and evaluated largely independently.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper unites LLM priors, episodic reflection-style memory, and MCTS/PUCT into a single algorithmic framework (MC-DML), providing an explicit algorithm, prompts, and ablation/empirical comparisons against RL, MCTS, and LLM baselines.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This concrete integration is a pragmatic unification (LLM priors + episodic reflections + tree search) that subsumes several prior approaches in one pipeline and demonstrates joint benefits empirically; it does not, however, claim or provide a formal theoretical unification with structured relational memory approaches (e.g., knowledge graphs) beyond empirical comparison.

unification.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: At the implementation level, the paper brings together structured persistent state (knowledge graphs) and ensembling/transfer via a Mixture-of-Experts with attention and a learnable expert, citing both KG-A2C and MoE/prior teacher-student literature. This combination is presented as the paper's method for few-shot role transfer.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory-level claim is an explicit unification: structured memory and episodic/reflection memory are complementary modules of a single memory architecture whose selective retrieval/composition is fundamental for scaling and generalization of LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not articulate or defend this higher-level unifying principle; at best its components can be interpreted as partial instances of such a unification, but the paper does not generalize or formalize memory-system unification across architectures or domains.

unification.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates CALM alongside prior strands (n-gram affordances, seq2seq in TextWorld, LM generation, and graph-augmented RL like KG-A2C) and empirically compares CALM to those baselines, but it treats them as alternative methods rather than as components of a single explanatory framework.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a unifying principle: LM priors, graph-based state representations, and episodic/trajectory-based learning should be viewed as complementary instantiations of a single underlying requirement—hybrid structured + episodic memory with selective retrieval—for robust LLM-agent performance.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper contains elements that touch each strand, but it does not explicitly synthesize them into the proposed unifying framework; such a synthesis can be inferred by combining insights across papers but is not articulated or validated here.

unification.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper builds on and combines prior components (knowledge-graph state representations from KG-A2C, soft prompts, and prompt-attention style fusion) into a single agent that can switch behaviors and perform few-shot blending.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a unifying principle: structured relational memory and episodic/reflection memory plus principled retrieval/composition are instances of a single underlying requirement for robust LLM-agent performance in complex, partially observable tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not claim or demonstrate this high-level conceptual unification across methods and phenomena; it demonstrates a practical composition of techniques for one domain but stops short of proposing or validating the general unifying principle.

unification.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper unifies imitation from demonstrations and interaction-generated successful trajectories under the same action-modeling objective (IAM), and contrasts its approach with other prior methods (CALM, BUTLER, Seq2Seq). It does not, however, discuss multiple memory representation families or subsume different memory modalities under a single principle.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly unifies structured relational memory and episodic/reflection memory into a single necessary framework for robust, generalizing LLM agents and makes retrieval/selectivity first-class in that unification.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This explicit conceptual unification of distinct memory modalities as jointly required for scaling and generalization is not present in the paper.

unification.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Reflexion unifies several prior components—self-refinement, in-context policy iteration, test-driven debugging, and episodic memory—into a single Actor–Evaluator–Self-Reflection framework and demonstrates cross-task applicability (decision-making, reasoning, programming).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly unifies diverse memory-augmentation paradigms by claiming they are instances of a two-modality principle: structured relational memory and episodic/reflection memory must cooperate via selective retrieval and composition for robust, scalable LLM agent behavior.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper unifies self-reflection approaches, it does not present or validate a unifying principle that subsumes structured-memory approaches together with episodic reflections as one required framework; that particular unification is absent from the paper.

unification.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates its pipeline among prior retrieval-augmented and LLM-agent works and empirically compares embedding retrieval to simpler baselines (TF-IDF, trigrams), but it frames contributions as engineering modules for a specific task rather than as a unifying theory.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly unifies multiple memory paradigms (structured relational stores, episodic traces, selective retrieval, and composition-with-reasoning) under a single claim that this hybrid memory and retrieval/composition design is essential for robust LLM agents in complex partially-observable games.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not provide a conceptual synthesis that subsumes graph-based memory, episodic replay, and reflective composition into one formal framework; that unifying claim goes beyond the paper's scope and would be new relative to the paper.

unification.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper positions Sweet&Sour as an extension of reflection-based methods (e.g., Reflexion) by incorporating positive experiences and managed memory, and it empirically compares these approaches to other baselines, but it does not claim to subsume or conceptually unify structured-memory literature.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly unifies two previously separate lines — structured relational memory (graphs/attributes/summaries) and episodic/reflection memory — into a single necessary framework for robust, long-horizon LLM agents, with selectivity/retrieval/composition as the linking principle.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This explicit unification is absent from the paper; the paper's contributions remain within episodic/reflection-style memory and do not integrate or treat structured relational-memory approaches as part of the same underlying principle.

unification.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies two previously separate tasks—knowledge-graph prediction and valid-action generation—into a single multi-task Worldformer architecture and further unifies representation/decoding by formulating both outputs as Sets-of-Sequences.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a higher-level unification: that structured relational memory and episodic/reflective memory (together with selective retrieval/composition) are instances of a single necessary memory principle for robust LLM-based agents across complex domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The cross-modality unification of structured and episodic memory as a single necessary organizing principle is not proposed or evaluated in the paper and thus is novel relative to this work.

unification.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper unifies prior ideas in one framework: prompting LLMs and using external symbolic modules to produce neurosymbolic behavior in text games, but it does not attempt to subsume different memory or replay approaches under a single organizing principle.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly unifies structured relational memory and episodic/reflection memory as complementary, necessary components of effective memory augmentation for LLM agents and places selective retrieval/composition at the center of that unification.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This explicit unification of disparate memory concepts into a single necessary-and-sufficient style principle is not present in the paper and therefore constitutes a novel unifying claim.

unification.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper relates diff history to existing abstraction techniques (e.g., Scratchpad, Chain-of-Thought) and positions delta-based history as a task-agnostic, procedural abstraction that can substitute for hand-crafted annotations in prompting/finetuning.
