dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly unifies two previously separate lines—structured memory representations (graphs, attributes, summaries) and episodic/reflection memory—claiming they are complementary components whose integration is a core requirement for robust LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not claim or demonstrate that diverse memory architectures (graph stores, episodic buffers, reflection processes) are instances of one underlying principle or must be integrated; that conceptual unification is not present in the paper and is a new, higher-level claim.

unification.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Within its scope, the paper explicitly unifies multiple distinct symbolic capabilities (calculator, navigation helper, sorter, KB lookup) under a single operational technique—action-space injection—and demonstrates a single model architecture (behavior-cloned transformer) can learn to call all of them from gold demonstrations. The related-work section situates this approach among prior neurosymbolic and graph-based methods.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory advances a higher-level unification claim that multiple memory/retrieval modalities (structured knowledge representations and episodic/reflection memory) are jointly required and should be integrated as a single organizing principle for robust LLM-based agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's explicit unification is limited to tool types → action-injection; it does not claim or demonstrate the broader unification of structured and episodic memory systems as equivalent or jointly necessary, so the theory's unification is not present in the paper.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The survey generalizes memory and reasoning design principles across six game genres and across modalities (textual, symbolic, visual), arguing for which memory design choices address which genre-specific demands (e.g., low-latency vs long-horizon).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It explicitly extends and recommends memory-structuring and selective retrieval approaches as broadly relevant across nontrivial, partially observable, long-horizon game settings, and it documents special-case caveats (trivial small tasks, fully random environments).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This is a literature-level generalization presented by the authors; it compiles and projects known techniques to broader settings but does not introduce a new mathematical generalization or proof.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: RAG and memory-augmented techniques have been primarily evaluated on knowledge-intensive NLP tasks or limited textual agent tasks; explicit application to embodied multimodal agents with visual observations and policy mapping has been comparatively under-explored.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: RAP extends retrieval-augmented episodic memory methods to multimodal embodied benchmarks (visual observations represented via CLIP/VLMs), demonstrates memory transfer across model pairs, and shows improvements across both text and visual-agent domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The empirical extension of retrieval-augmented episodic memory to multimodal embodied decision-making and the demonstration of cross-model memory reuse materially broadens the domain of these techniques and is validated in the paper.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Knowledge-graph methods have been applied primarily to static QA/document tasks and episodic approaches to isolated agent reflections; prior LLM-agent memory work often evaluated on narrower environment sets or single memory modalities.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper demonstrates AriGraph across diverse domains (several TextWorld tasks of increasing difficulty, NetHack partial-observation experiments, and multi-hop QA), arguing the approach generalizes beyond static QA to interactive, partially observable, long-horizon tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This constitutes an empirical scope expansion showing applicability of hybrid KG+episodic memory to interactive POMDP-style text games and to retrieval Q&A, though the claim is demonstrated experimentally rather than proven theoretically.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent based on Theory of Mind in Guandan (搜蛋): A Multi-Player Cooperative Game under Imperfect Information

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work has shown ToM prompting and tool use can help LLM agents in simpler or two-player settings (mostly English), and that filtering/candidate selection can aid decision systems in other domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: An explicit empirical generalization: ToM-based planning combined with an external action-recommender improves LLM agent performance in a harder, four-player cooperative imperfect-information Chinese card game (Guandan) across multiple LLM families and prompt orders.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The contribution is an applied scope expansion demonstrating these interventions transfer to a non-English, multi-player, long-horizon environment; it is an empirical extension rather than a novel theoretical generalization.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The authors fine-tune DistilBERT on a large set of human gameplay transcripts (ClubFloyd) distinct from evaluation games and evaluate the same LM-augmented agents across 14 diverse Jericho games of varying difficulty, reporting cross-game performance and a limited cross-game transfer test (Zork1→Zork3).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper provides evidence that a single LM instance, adapted to game language via transcript fine-tuning, generalizes across multiple human-written IF games and reduces required environment interactions compared to several baselines.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper explicitly demonstrates across-game generalization within the IF/Jericho domain, it does not claim or show that this generalization extends beyond similar IF-style, text-adventure environments or to modalities beyond text; the scope expansion is empirical and domain-limited.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Experiments are limited to one newly designed non-cooperative game (Goedendag) at two board scales (4x4 and 10x10); authors mention possible extensions (cooperative games, memory bank) but provide no cross-domain empirical validation.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory claims broad scope: that hybrid structured–episodic memory and selective retrieval are necessary for robust scaling and generalization of LLM agents across partially observable, long-horizon, branching text-game domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides limited empirical support for generalization and only suggests memory-bank extensions; the broad, cross-domain generalization claim of the theory is not demonstrated and thus remains an unproven extrapolation (derivable in principle but unstated and unvalidated here).

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The experiments and claims in the paper are scoped to text role-playing/adventure games in the LIGHT environment and to models built on KG-A2C-style architectures; results show benefits in these settings (character disentanglement, few-shot blending).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory extends the claim to a much broader scope: that memory structure, selectivity, and retrieval/composition are as critical as memory size or modality for any LLM-based agent operating in nontrivial partially-observable, long/branching-horizon domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the task-specific findings to a domain-general necessity statement is not established by this paper; the paper's results make the generalization plausible but do not provide cross-domain evidence or theoretical proof.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper's empirical claims are specific to the ALFWorld benchmark and GPT2-medium models with up to a 1000-token context; it briefly speculates about few-shot priming and broader trends but offers no empirical or theoretical claims that generalize to arbitrary long-horizon, highly-branching text-game domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory extends the claim to a broad scope: for any LLM-based agent operating in nontrivial state/action spaces with partial observability and long or branching horizons, hybrid structured–episodic memory and selective retrieval are required for robust scaling and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That broad, cross-environment scope is not argued or demonstrated by the paper; while the paper's context-window limits make such issues plausible, the necessity claim across domains is not established and must be inferred beyond the paper's results.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates that distilled verbal self-reflection generalizes across multiple task families (ALFWorld, HotPotQA, HumanEval/MBPP/Leetcode) and notes practical limits (small bounded mem windows, context-size constraints) and potential future memory backends (vector DBs, SQL).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory expands the scope by claiming the Reflexion-style benefits should extend only as part of a hybrid structured+episodic memory architecture with selective retrieval/composition, and that this hybrid is necessary across complex, partially observable, long-horizon domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending Reflexion’s episodic-memory result to a required hybrid structured+episodic architecture is an extrapolation not empirically tested in the paper; the hybrid-as-necessary claim is a scope expansion derivable from but not demonstrated by the paper's experiments or analyses.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates benefits of retrieval plus reflection/verification in the Jubensha narrative multi-agent domain, including experiments with full-script access and ablations across module combinations and LLM capabilities (GPT-3.5 vs GPT-4).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes this requirement to all LLM-based agents operating in nontrivial state/action spaces with partial observability and long/branching planning horizons, claiming that hybrid structured-episodic memory and selective retrieval are necessary for scaling and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides domain-specific empirical evidence but does not test across diverse environments, modalities, or structured-memory implementations; the broad cross-domain generalization is asserted by the theory but not demonstrated or derived in the paper.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reports results from a single, comprehensive environment (ScienceWorld) and states Sweet&Sour is broadly applicable in principle, but it does not provide multi-environment empirical validation or formal generalization guarantees.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory expands the claim to a domain-wide requirement: across complex, partially observable, long- or branching-horizon text-game settings, memory structure, selectivity, and retrieval/composition (including hybrid structured-episodic memory) are essential for scaling and generalization beyond mere memory volume or naive history retention.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the paper's single-environment managed-episodic finding to a domain-wide necessary condition for hybrid structured-episodic memory is not established by the paper; the broader scope claim goes beyond the presented experiments and is not explicitly derived there.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper shows that the Worldformer components (graph-diff prediction, multi-tasking, SOS loss) generalize across multiple unseen text games within the domain of interactive fiction, indicating improved zero-shot transfer among similar textual environments.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes beyond the paper by claiming that hybrid structured–episodic memory and principled selective retrieval are required for robust performance and scaling of LLM-based agents in any nontrivial partially observable, long-horizon domain.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the paper's in-domain empirical findings to a broad claim about all LLM-based agents and domains is not supported by the experiments; this broader scope expansion is not established here and is a novel generalization.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates the architecture only in one engineered domain (the Smallville sandbox) with 25 agents over two simulated days, and while the authors argue the primitives are likely to generalize as models improve, they explicitly acknowledge limited timescale, domain, and cost constraints.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory extends the claim to a broad scope: that hybrid structured-episodic memory with selective retrieval is a general requirement for robust performance across LLM-based agents operating in any nontrivial state/action space with partial observability and long/branching horizons.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That cross-domain, broadly-scoped requirement is not empirically demonstrated in the paper; it is a plausible and natural generalization from the paper's results but remains an unproven extrapolation beyond the single-domain experiments.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior LLM-planning results were typically shown on simpler or more structured tasks; methods that scale RL or KG-based representations to complex Jericho-style games required iterative learning/large training budgets.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper extends LLM-guided planning to long-horizon, partially observable, high-branching text games (Jericho suite) and shows that memory-guided LLM priors produce strong initial-planning performance without multi-iteration planning-then-learning cycles; it also documents implementation choices (memory sizes, truncation, dynamic pruning) needed to operate within prompt-size limits.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Empirical scope expansion to hard, long-horizon text games with bounded reflection memory and dynamic pruning is new in practice, demonstrating that these design choices enable LLM+MCTS to be effective in this domain.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper evaluates action-injection across four parametrically varied benchmark families in partially observable, multi-step text-game settings and shows reproducible improvements for the behavior-cloned transformer; it also notes settings where module actions increase training cost or action-space size and acknowledges trivial cases where modules add little value.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes to a broad claim that in any nontrivial state/action-space, partially observable, long-horizon domain, robust generalization requires a hybrid of structured and episodic memory with selective retrieval and composition rather than mere memory volume or naive concatenation.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides task-limited empirical support that structured external tools help sequence models in text games, but it neither evaluates episodic/reflection memory mechanisms nor demonstrates the proposed hybrid requirement across domains; the broader scope is plausible but not established by the paper and would require additional theoretical/empirical work to substantiate.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper's experiments and claims are explicitly scoped to ScienceWorld (10 elementary-school science tasks) and four agent types (DRRN, KG-A2C, RoBERTa, Swift) with specific injection strategies and ablations; results are reported per task and per model.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes from these results to a broad domain claim: that hybrid structured-episodic memory with selective retrieval is a fundamental requirement for robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility for LLM-based agents in complex, partially observable, long-horizon/text-game-like settings (and implicitly beyond).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This domain-level generalization (elevating empirical findings from one environment and limited agent classes to a general requirement across LLM-agent settings) is not established by the paper and represents a substantive scope expansion beyond its evidence.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates that the KG + template + graph-mask + valid-action supervision approach generalizes across a diverse set of 28 human-authored IF games, arguing broad applicability within the interactive-fiction domain and across different game genres and structures.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes this result far beyond IF: it claims that for any LLM-based agent operating in nontrivial, partially observable, long- or branching-horizon environments, hybrid structured-episodic memory with selective retrieval is a fundamental requirement for robust generalization and strategic flexibility.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That domain-agnostic, task-agnostic general requirement (moving from IF-game evidence to a general law for LLM agents across modalities and training regimes) is not supported or tested by the paper and therefore represents a substantive scope expansion.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Experiments show that MemInsight's augmentation + selective retrieval generalizes across three dialogue-centered tasks and multiple LLM backbones within multi-session conversational datasets (LLM-REDIAL, LoCoMo).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory extends the claim beyond dialogues: it asserts that hybrid structured–episodic memory is a fundamental requirement for robust performance in LLM-based agents operating in complex, partially observable, long-horizon, branching text-game environments.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending necessity claims to long-horizon, partially observable planning/text-game settings goes beyond the empirical domain tested in the paper (dialogue datasets) and is not supported or evaluated there, so this scope expansion is novel relative to the paper.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates generalization empirically within TextWorld: a single GATA policy trained across many sampled games generalizes better to unseen games and difficulty mixes than text-only baselines, and ground-truth graphs raise the upper bound.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory extends the claim to a much broader scope: that hybrid structured-episodic memory and selective retrieval are fundamental requirements for robust performance of LLM-based agents across nontrivial partially observable, long-horizon domains (beyond TextWorld).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides domain-specific evidence (text games) but does not empirically or theoretically establish the cross-domain/LLM-scale generalization or the claim of fundamental necessity; extending the claim beyond the paper's experimental regime is novel relative to this work.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper's empirical scope is limited to four symbolic text-game benchmarks (Arithmetic, MapReader, Sorting, TWC) and primarily evaluates prompt+module integration; it only briefly suggests extending to more complex domains without formal claims about general architectural requirements.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes to a broader domain: for LLM-based agents operating in any nontrivial state/action space with partial observability and long/branching planning horizons, robust generalization requires hybrid structured-episodic memory plus selective retrieval rather than mere increases in context window or raw episodic logs.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This broad scope-expansion claim is not argued or demonstrated by the paper's experiments and therefore represents a genuinely new generalization beyond the paper.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates diff history benefits across two procedurally generated text environments (BabyAI-Text and NetHack) and characterizes when diff helps or hurts (structured observations vs. narrative/unstructured text).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory expands the scope to assert that for all LLM-based agents operating in nontrivial state/action POMDP-like settings with long or branching horizons, success depends on hybrid structured-episodic memory and selective retrieval/composition rather than merely increasing memory size or sticking to one modality.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper provides cross-environment supportive evidence for structured, selective representations, it does not empirically or analytically establish the universal scope claimed by the theory; the global scope expansion is an extrapolation beyond the tested settings.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper's scope and experiments are limited to KG-A2C agents in a LIGHT-based open-ended text-roleplaying environment and to mixtures of frozen role-specific experts plus one trainable expert; it does not evaluate large pretrained LLMs, multimodal agents, or broad classes of partially-observable tasks beyond their environment.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes the claim to all LLM-based agents operating in partially observable, long-horizon, branching domains, stating that hybrid structured–episodic memory with selective retrieval is a fundamental requirement for robust scaling and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the paper's specific KG+MoE result to a general requirement for all LLM-based agents and tasks is not supported by the experiments or analysis in the paper and therefore constitutes a substantive scope expansion beyond the paper's evidence.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates cross-game generalization of a single CALM model trained on diverse human transcripts to unseen Jericho games, and studies robustness across 28 man-made games and several decoding/ablation settings (k, pretraining, dataset subsets).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes beyond those experiments to claim an architectural requirement for all LLM-based agents in partially observable, long-horizon, branching text environments: that scaling and generalization depend more on memory structure and selective retrieval than on raw memory size or unstructured modality.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's empirical generalization (single LM generalizing across games) is a narrower result; the stronger claim that hybrid structured-episodic memory is broadly necessary across domains and scales is not supported or tested by the paper and therefore represents a scope expansion beyond the presented evidence.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: It is established that naively concatenating long histories into prompts is limited by context-window size and can introduce noise/irrelevant information, degrading performance in long-horizon or partially observable tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper operationalizes this limitation for agents and prescribes concrete mitigations: adaptive weighting of similarity components, modality-aware retrieval, and returning only a centered window of trajectory steps around the most-similar action to avoid information overload.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the high-level limitation is known, RAP's concrete mitigation strategy (retrieval keys + weighted similarity + windowed trajectory composition calibrated by environment) is explicitly documented and ablated empirically.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly shows constraints: naive direct querying or single-model CoT yields higher error rates and cannot efficiently handle exponentially many possible states; area selection is presented to limit memory growth from exponential to linear in the selected subset.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: Both the paper and the theory claim that unstructured full-history concatenation or brute-force context-window approaches fail to scale and produce information overload or irrelevant context in complex games.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The limitation of naive full-history prompting and the benefit of selective, structured state selection are explicitly established by the paper and therefore not novel relative to its content.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper enumerates limitations reported across studies: working-memory capacity limits, temporal inconsistency from stochastic reasoning traces, latency trade-offs between deep reasoning and real-time control, serialization bottlenecks in multi-agent pipelines, and overhead of structured memory in unstructured/random environments.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It synthesizes these failure regimes and architectural constraints as explicit design considerations for LLMGAs, clarifying when memory augmentation helps and when it yields little marginal benefit.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: These constraints are collated from existing empirical and engineering reports and are presented explicitly; the survey formalizes them as central limitations but does not claim previously unknown failure modes.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reports trade-offs and failure modes: naive full-history prompt concatenation is token-inefficient and does not scale, single-triplet observations have low episodic retrieval value, graph construction/update costs and token usage are nontrivial, and benefits shrink when history fits in the prompt or environments are random.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It specifies practical constraints and heuristics (e.g., do not replace distinct informational triplets, episodic relevance scoring formula, token-usage comparisons, and special-case behavior in trivial or fully random environments).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The empirical characterization of these limitations and the concrete operational heuristics for updating/retrieving in a dynamic KG+episodic system are documented contributions that clarify when hybrid structure helps or hurts.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly identifies limitations and failure regimes: adding affordances as raw concatenated strings can degrade performance, MCA yields inconsistent gains and is task-dependent, and affordances irrelevant to a task (or missing in the KG) provide little benefit; trivial small tasks derive little from advanced retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory restates and generalizes a constraint: pure unstructured retention or naive episodic-only strategies are insufficient for scaling or generalization and selective, structured access is necessary to avoid overload and sparsity.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The specific constraint that unstructured concatenation and naive memory methods fail (and that KG-based structured injection mitigates this) is directly observed and analyzed in the paper; the theory's generalization of this limitation is a logical extension rather than a new failure mode.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly documents limitations of unstructured or naive memory: raw historical context grows noisy and large, naive concatenation/all-history retrieval is inefficient and degrades performance, and unstructured memory hinders cross-context integration.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory states as a constraint that naive retention or pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory will fail to scale or generalize in complex tasks, and that selective structuring and retrieval are necessary to balance compression, relevance, and reasoning depth.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This limitation — that unstructured memory and brute-force context concatenation are problematic and that structured/selective retrieval mitigates those problems — is already explicit and empirically supported in the paper.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly identifies constraints and failure modes: limited LM context windows prevent naively including full history, naive concatenation or brute-force context produces distraction/overload, retrieval failures and hallucinations occur, and instruction tuning can bias or oversmooth behavior. It documents these limitations in qualitative analysis and failure cases.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory emphasizes as a constraint that memory volume or unstructured retention alone is insufficient and can cause information overload or sparsity, and that selective structuring and retrieval are necessary to balance compression, relevance, and reasoning depth when scaling to hard tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Those specific constraints and failure regimes of naive approaches are explicitly discussed and empirically observed in the paper; the theory's constraint emphasis mostly restates and generalizes limitations already shown by the authors.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly documents failure modes: a baseline KG-A2C trained jointly on conflicting character rewards suffers entangled behavior and degraded per-character performance, certain attention placements (over prompts or probabilities) fail in ablations, and unfrozen fine-tuning on a new character causes catastrophic forgetting.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader constraint that naive unstructured retention (e.g., brute-force context concatenation) or pure unstructured episodic memory without selective retrieval/structure will not scale or generalize in complex long-horizon tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides empirical examples of related failure modes at task scale, but it does not empirically or theoretically establish the general impossibility/necessity claim for all LLM-agent settings; the paper's limitations support the constraint but do not elevate it to a general law.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly documents several constraints and failure modes: context-window limits force bounding memory (they use last 1–3 reflections), naive unbounded concatenation of histories is impractical, Reflexion can fail on high-exploration tasks (WebShop) and is sensitive to evaluator/test quality (false positives/negatives in generated unit tests).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory frames a stronger constraint: that unstructured retention or pure episodic memory alone will not scale or generalize in many complex, long-horizon, partially observable environments and that selective, structured retrieval/composition is required to avoid overload, irrelevance, or sparsity.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper supplies empirical and practical constraints that support the importance of representation and selectivity, but it does not formally demonstrate that unstructured episodic memory alone is universally insufficient nor compare structured alternatives; the stronger universal limitation claim is asserted by the theory but only peripherally supported by the paper.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper documents practical constraints: limited context windows motivate external retrieval, naive baselines (No MR, TF-IDF, trigrams) perform substantially worse than embedding retrieval, and Full Script Access gives an upper bound on performance in their tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts stronger constraints: that naive retention of whole history or reliance on purely unstructured episodic memory will fail to scale or generalize in complex games, and that selective, structured retrieval/composition is necessary to avoid overload or sparsity.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper shows context-window limits and baseline failures in this setting, it does not systematically evaluate brute-force concatenation at large scale nor characterize the failure regimes of purely unstructured episodic stores versus structured selective schemes; the theory's constraint claims extend beyond the paper's empirical coverage.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent based on Theory of Mind in Guandan (搜蛋): A Multi-Player Cooperative Game under Imperfect Information

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper documents clear limitations of LLM agents: without targeted intervention they fail to match SOTA RL in this domain, are sensitive to player position, and are impaired by very long/dynamic action lists and naïve concatenation of history.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: A concrete failure regime is identified and quantified: unstructured concatenation of long action lists/history leads to information overload and front-of-list bias that degrades planning and decision quality for LLM agents in complex multi-agent games.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper empirically quantifies this limitation in a realistic multi-agent cooperative environment and shows selective retrieval (top-k) alleviates but does not always eliminate the gap to RL baselines.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: It is known that full-history concatenation is infeasible for LLMs due to context window limits, that LLMs alone can fail to balance exploration/exploitation, and that some puzzles require long-range recall beyond short windows.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper explicitly documents practical constraints: (i) defining in-trial memory as a short window can miss distant clues (needle-in-haystack), (ii) limiting cross-trial reflections (k=3) is necessary for prompt-budget reasons, and (iii) dynamic pruning and truncation are required to keep search tractable; ablations quantify performance drops when memory modules are removed.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: These limitations are explicitly acknowledged and empirically characterized within this algorithmic context, but they mostly restate practical trade-offs that were previously understood in the community rather than revealing a fundamentally new failure regime.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly documents practical constraints: a finite context window (max sequence length 1000), use of a sliding window that truncates history, and dependence of performance on number of demonstrations (Figure 2), but it does not systematically analyze failure modes of unstructured concatenation in large or branching tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a specific limitation: unstructured brute-force concatenation or naive retention of all history leads to information overload or sparse, irrelevant context and will fail to scale in complex games unless memory is structured and retrieval is selective.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper implicitly points to token-limit constraints that make unstructured approaches bounded, it does not empirically characterize the suggested failure regimes nor demonstrate that structured/selective memory would resolve them; the limitation statement is not made explicit or tested in the paper.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically identifies failure regimes and constraints: decoding over the full vocabulary (unconstrained word-level action spaces) and removing valid-action supervision result in drastically worse performance or failure to learn, showing that naively exploring the full NL action space is infeasible without structure or auxiliary signals.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory-level constraint asserts more generally that naive retention of all history or sole reliance on unstructured episodic memory will not scale or generalize in complex, long-horizon partially observable settings; instead, selective retrieval and structured representations are necessary to avoid information overload or sparsity.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper explicitly shows failure of unconstrained decoding and the utility of selectivity/structure, it does not test or rule out sophisticated episodic-only systems; therefore the absolute claim that pure episodic/unstructured memory is insufficient is a novel limitation beyond the paper's evidence (it is plausible but not demonstrated).

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper identifies limitations of prior reflection methods (they learn only from failures, fail when agents are initially successful, perform worse with smaller LLMs, and depend on external feedback) and empirically shows failure-only sampling can degrade performance. It also notes evaluation is limited to one environment.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a specific constraint: naive unstructured retention (brute-force context-window concatenation or pure unstructured episodic logs) will produce information overload or sparsity and fail to scale; selective structure and retrieval are necessary to avoid these failure regimes in complex tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides partial evidence of limitations of failure-only reflection and sensitivity to model size but does not experimentally analyze or demonstrate the explicit failure regimes claimed for unstructured brute-force memory versus structured/selective retrieval at scale.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper documents constraints and failure modes in its domain: unstructured/extractive Seq2Seq approaches are data inefficient and prone to over-extraction or noisy predictions, and predicting full next-state graphs is substantially harder than predicting graph differences; ablations quantify performance drops when structural components are removed.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader limitation: naive retention of all history or pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory will fail to scale or generalize in complex, long-horizon LLM-agent settings, and selective, structured retrieval/composition is necessary to avoid information overload or sparsity.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper explicitly establishes that unstructured sequence approaches perform poorly in text games, the stronger, domain-general limitation (that episodic-only systems will not scale across LLM agents broadly) is not established here and extends beyond the paper's evidence.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper documents limitations: LM augmentation mitigates but does not eliminate exploration failures or long-horizon search problems, and trained DBERT does not fully solve discovering some treasures or finding highest-score trajectories without improved exploration strategies.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It empirically shows that supplying LM priors reduces some failure modes (e.g., missing affordance-based actions) but that remaining bottlenecks are exploration and learning strategies rather than pure language encoding, and it cautions that training the LM on unrelated or random environments would bring little benefit.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The specific theory claim that naive unstructured retention (brute-force context-window concatenation) fails to scale and that selective structured+episodic retrieval is required is not analyzed or demonstrated in this paper; the paper reports related practical limitations but does not isolate memory-structure vs. memory-size failure regimes.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reports relevant limitations: discrete updater approaches can suffer from error accumulation and brittleness, learned continuous belief graphs are denser/noisier than ground truth, and text-only recurrent agents provide limited implicit memory compared to structured belief-graph architectures; it also notes trivial/small settings where advanced retrieval gives little gain.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a general constraint: naive retention strategies (brute-force prompt concatenation or pure unstructured episodic memory) fail to scale or generalize in complex, long-horizon, partially observable text tasks, so selective structure and retrieval are necessary.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides partial, task-specific evidence consistent with limitations of naive/unstructured approaches (text-only baselines underperform), but it does not systematically evaluate or prove the broad failure mode claim that brute-force context window or pure unstructured episodic memory cannot scale across the class of LLM-based, long-horizon partially observable tasks.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper documents concrete limitations: agents forget route information (MapReader), have constrained memory capacity that harms sorting, and benefit from constrained prompts; it notes that learning rules solely via environment interaction is difficult in these tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory identifies a specific limitation regime: unstructured brute-force retention (naive concatenation of history or simply enlarging context) leads to information overload or sparsity and will not scale in complex, partially observable, long-horizon tasks unless supplanted by structured storage and selective retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper reports memory-related failures and that constrained prompting helps, it does not systematically characterize or establish the failure mode 'unstructured retention versus selective structured retrieval' as a general necessary limitation; that characterization is only implicitly suggested and not analyzed.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly documents failure modes and constraints: naive concatenation of full-text history can overwhelm models (dramatic drop in NetHack), diff can increase token count and overhead in some low-dimensional tasks, and diff is unlikely to help when observations lack exploitable structure without preprocessing.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader limitation: neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone suffices for scaling and generalization in complex tasks; selective structured storage plus episodic/reflection mechanisms are necessary to avoid overload and sparsity.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper establishes concrete, environment-specific constraints and shows that structure/selection matter in practice; however, the stronger general claim that pure episodic/unstructured memory will generally be insufficient across the class of complex LLM-agent problems is not empirically established and is an inferred general limitation.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly documents key practical constraints: reliance on a privileged valid-action aid, dependence on gold trajectories for behavior cloning, action-space blowup when many module actions are injected (notably the KB module), and RL-specific failure modes due to exploration and myopic rewards. These limitations are discussed in a dedicated Limitations section.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a conceptual constraint that unstructured brute-force retention (e.g., naive prompt concatenation) or pure unstructured episodic memory will fail to scale or generalize, and that selective, structured retrieval/composition is necessary to avoid overload or sparsity.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's documented constraints provide circumstantial support for the need for selective mechanisms (action-space growth, gold-data dependence), but it does not directly compare naive unstructured memory strategies versus structured/selective memory designs or prove the asserted failure regimes; thus the specific constraint claim is not directly tested.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly documents several limitations and failure regimes of its approach: premature convergence and expert starvation due to value-seeding, sensitivity to adversarial/irrelevant experts that can hinder exploration, and effective degradation to scratch training when no relevant experts exist; it also presents mitigation strategies (epsilon-greedy, auxiliary loss).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader limitation: naive retention of entire history or pure unstructured episodic concatenation (brute-force context window) will not scale and leads to overload or sparsity, so selective retrieval and structured representations are necessary.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper offers concrete constraints related to expert mixing and attention dynamics but does not empirically or analytically evaluate the specific failure mode of unstructured brute-force history retention versus structured selective retrieval across long horizons, so that particular limitation is not demonstrated in the paper.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper documents practical limitations: off-the-shelf LMs without adaptation produce many unparsable actions; small k misses admissible/gold actions and hurts performance; increasing k raises recall but lowers precision, creating an exploration/exploitation tradeoff; and DRRN sometimes fails to exploit high-scoring trajectories available under CALM.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory promulgates a broader limitation claim: naive retention of full history or sole reliance on unstructured episodic memory (prompt concatenation / brute-force context windows) will not scale or generalize in complex, long-horizon partially observable tasks, because they cause information overload or sparsity without selective structured retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper illustrates several specific failure modes of naive generation and context choices but does not formulate or empirically validate the general, necessity-style limitation that unstructured episodic memory alone is insufficient; that broader negative claim is stronger than the paper's observed constraints and is not established here.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes LLM-based game agents through a cognitive-inspired three-module architecture (memory, reasoning, perception–action) and further decomposes memory into working vs long-term with sub-processes (consolidation, structuring, retrieval).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: This conceptual abstraction and the genre–challenge taxonomy reorganize disparate prior contributions into an actionable design vocabulary for LLMGAs, making comparative evaluation and design discussion more systematic.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The reframing is an explicit organizational contribution of the survey—useful and clarifying but built from existing concepts rather than introducing a wholly new theoretical abstraction.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Cognitive notions of semantic vs. episodic memory are known in prior work, and some agent works use separate episodic logs or KGs, but a succinct, implementable formalization combining them as a four-part graph is not widespread in LLM-agent literature.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper reframes agent memory as a unified graph abstraction G = (V_s,E_s,V_e,E_e) that operationalizes semantic/episodic distinctions and integrates them with planning and retrieval primitives.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Providing this explicit graph-level abstraction (including episodic edges that bind triplets to observations) as the central organizing concept for LLM agent memory is a clear conceptual contribution and practical design pattern.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Agent architectures have previously been discussed in modular terms (e.g., memory, planning, action) and retrieval-as-augmentation is an established abstraction in NLP.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: RAP frames episodic memory usage for agents via the retrieval-key abstraction (action- or observation-driven keys) and a four-module pipeline that treats retrieval selection and composition as first-class, context-sensitive operations in the agent loop.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This modular framing and the explicit retrieval-key abstraction recast how episodic experiences are represented and queried in LLM agents; the reframing is practical and applied rather than a deep theoretical overhaul.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper frames an applied 'knowledge injection' taxonomy separating action-memory (MCA) and affordances and enumerates injection strategies (add to KG, separate encoder, concat, pretrain auxiliary QA), arguing that structured KG injection is superior for affordances.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory offers a higher-level conceptual reframing: treat agent memory as a hybrid structured-episodic architecture with selective retrieval/composition as the central organizing abstraction for robust reasoning and generalization in LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper presents a practical two-part taxonomy and shows method-level differences, but it does not elevate these results into the broader, principled hybrid-memory abstraction with selective-composition as a central organizing theory; that reframing is only partially present and remains peripheral in the paper.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent based on Theory of Mind in Guandan (搜蛋): A Multi-Player Cooperative Game under Imperfect Information

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper proposes a modular agent architecture (State Interpreter, ToM Planner, Plan Evaluator) and frames ToM as planning orders (zero/first/second) that can be added to the planning loop.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: A pragmatic reframing that treats belief inference (ToM) and external tool invocation (action recommender) as distinct modular components feeding a prompt-based planner/evaluator pipeline.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This is a useful organizational abstraction for building LLM agents in multi-agent imperfect-information settings, but it is an incremental engineering reframing rather than a fundamentally new conceptual paradigm about memory or retrieval architectures.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper frames pre-trained LMs as modular priors (linguistic, world, game-sense) that can be plugged into RL agents to improve semantic understanding and reduce sample complexity, positioning this module as an alternative or complement to engineered structured representations.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: This reframing emphasizes leveraging unsupervised, large-scale language pretraining plus light task-specific transcript fine-tuning as a practical ingredient for language-grounded RL agents rather than relying solely on engineered state graphs or heavy task-specific representation learning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The reframing is explicit but modest: it advocates a modular LM-as-prior design pattern for IF agents but does not introduce a new abstract memory decomposition (e.g., formal hybrid structured vs episodic memory architecture) nor operational mechanisms for selective retrieval/composition.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes LLM-agent design away from single-shot prompting toward an architectural view that treats memory management (a natural-language memory stream), retrieval, reflection, and planning as core, reusable primitives for long-term coherence and believable behavior. It also introduces the idea of reflection trees and environment subgraphs as architectural artifacts.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory offers a high-level reframing that foregrounds a hybrid taxonomy—structured relational memory (graphs/attributes) plus episodic/reflection memory—with selective retrieval and composition as the central abstraction governing agent robustness and generalization tradeoffs.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides a substantial conceptual reframing (memory + retrieval + reflection + planning) and implements an environment tree; however, it does not formalize a general 'hybrid structured-episodic' taxonomy or deeply analyze tradeoffs between formal relational representations and unstructured episodic text, so the theory's particular taxonomy and emphasis are an extension but closely related to the paper's framing.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior works discussed memory, reflection, and priors for planning but did not consistently separate their roles inside a search-based planning loop for text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper frames memory for LLM-guided planning as two distinct functional modalities—in-trial short-term trajectory memory for immediate conditioning and cross-trial episodic reflections for enduring corrective signals—and treats the LLM as a dynamic prior generator whose outputs are composed into the PUCT term.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This explicit two-tier memory abstraction and its placement as an online modifier of the prior inside MCTS is a useful organizational lens and operational design pattern, novel in how it prescribes their distinct roles inside tree search, though conceptually it synthesizes rather than overturns prior perspectives.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Reflexion introduces a conceptual reframing: policy improvement can be achieved via 'verbal' reinforcement where distilled natural-language self-reflections serve as persistent in-context memory, operationalized as an Actor–Evaluator–Self-Reflection abstraction that separates short-term trajectories from long-term distilled experiences.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes memory design as a composable, two-layer abstraction (structured relational stores + episodic/reflection traces) and elevates retrieval/selectivity/composition mechanisms to first-class design primitives that are as critical as memory capacity or modality.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already advances a notable reframing (verbal reinforcement and persisted reflective memory) but does not introduce or evaluate the two-layer structured-vs-episodic abstraction or explicitly elevate retrieval/composition as coequal core abstractions; that reframing is a novel extension relative to this paper.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper proposes the ThinkThrice abstraction (Memory Retrieval → Self-Refinement → Self-Verification) as an organizing pipeline for multi-agent text-game agents, which is a concrete conceptual framing for retrieval + reflective agent design.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes memory design as a principled hybrid between structured relational memory (graphs/attributes/summaries) and episodic/reflection memory, elevating selective retrieval and composition rules to be as central as memory size or modality for robust agent behavior.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: There is partial overlap: the paper's ThinkThrice pipeline embodies episodic retrieval and reflective verification, but it does not articulate or operationalize an explicit structured-relational memory component or the hybrid abstraction the theory proposes; recasting the paper's modules in the hybrid structured-vs-episodic lens is possible but not performed by the authors.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper introduces the 'belief graph' abstraction: a learned, dynamic graph-structured belief state that acts as structured memory for partially observable text-game environments and specifies updater, encoder, aggregator, and scorer modules.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a reframing: treat agent memory as a hybrid abstraction ('Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory') that explicitly integrates relational, compact structured representations with episodic/reflection mechanisms and selective retrieval/composition as the organizing lens for robust LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The belief-graph abstraction in the paper supplies one half of this reframing (structured memory); the explicit hybrid abstraction that elevates episodic/reflection as a distinct, necessary complement and a new organizing principle is not developed or argued in the paper, so the conceptual reframing is novel with respect to this work.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper frames few-shot task-transfer in role-playing text worlds as a practical architecture: frozen role-aligned experts + a trainable expert + attention + KG persistent state; this is an applied architectural framing rather than a general theory of memory.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes memory design for LLM agents at an abstract level: treat memory as a principled hybrid of structured (relational) and episodic (reflective) subsystems, with selective retrieval/composition as a first-class design principle for scaling and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Although components of the paper instantiate parts of that abstraction (KG as structured memory, hot expert as adaptive component), the paper does not articulate or promote the higher-level conceptual reframing of memory-systems design as a general organizing principle.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes LLM-based game solving as a modular, multi-agent pipeline (task decomposition plus tree-of-thought branching) and frames area selection as an explicit mechanism to control state-space and memory growth.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes memory for LLM agents as a hybrid abstraction combining structured relational memory and episodic/reflection memory with selective retrieval/composition as the central organizing design principle for robustness and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's conceptual framing differs: it proposes task decomposition and bounded-area selection but does not formalize or adopt the hybrid structured–episodic memory abstraction as an organizing principle; that higher-level reframing is therefore novel with respect to this paper.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper introduces conceptual distinctions useful to the domain: 'character agent' vs 'actor/thespian agent', soft character prompts as embedding-level instructions, and the abstraction of stacked per-character logits that can be composed.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent memory design as an explicit hybrid abstraction—structured relational memory plus episodic/reflection memory—with retrieval/selectivity/composition interfaces treated as first-order design variables critical to robustness and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper presents building blocks that align with this reframing, it does not present or argue for the higher-level, memory-centric abstraction as an organizing principle; that conceptual reframing is not present and would be a novel contribution beyond the paper.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper treats persistent memory operationally as the concatenated textual context (with modality markers) and frames the solution as improved training (imitation + IAM) rather than proposing a new abstract memory architecture.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent memory as an architectural abstraction: a hybrid system combining a compact structured relational store and an episodic/reflection store with selective retrieval and composition as core design primitives that determine robustness and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This organizing abstraction and reframing of memory as a hybrid structured–episodic system with retrieval/composition as first-class operations is not present in the paper and represents a new conceptual lens relative to its engineering description.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper frames the IF agent problem as a joint state-action representation challenge and operationalizes an abstraction: knowledge graphs as learned structured state memory and template-based actions (with graph masking) as an actionable, structured generation abstraction.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes memory requirements at a higher conceptual level: it proposes the organizing principle that robust LLM-based agents should be designed around a hybrid memory architecture (structured relational memory plus episodic/reflection memory) with principled selective retrieval and composition as a core abstraction.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This higher-level reframing—treating hybrid structured+episodic memory and selective composition as the central organizing design principle for LLM agents—is not articulated in the paper and represents a new conceptual abstraction beyond the paper's state-action coupling.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers a conceptual reframing within the reflection literature: Sweet&Sour reframes self-reflection to include positive experiences and formalizes a managed-memory abstraction (short-term buffer moved to long-term storing reflection tuples) rather than naive persistent logs.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a higher-level abstraction that agent memory should be treated as a hybrid of complementary modalities (structured relational memory and episodic/reflection memory) whose controlled composition and selective retrieval — not raw size or modality alone — are the central design principle for robust LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Recasting memory as a required hybrid abstraction with structured-relational and episodic modalities is a conceptual step beyond the paper's managed-episodic reframing; that hybrid abstraction is not present or argued for in the paper.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: MemInsight reframes agent memory practically as attribute-centric augmentations parameterized by perspective (entity vs conversation), granularity (turn vs session), and prioritization, and separates retrieval into comprehensive vs refined modes; this is a concrete conceptual abstraction for building memory pipelines.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes memory at a higher abstraction: it posits that a hybrid decomposition (structured relational memory + episodic/reflection store) and their selective composition during retrieval should be regarded as a fundamental architectural abstraction for robust LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper proposes and formalizes an attribute-augmentation abstraction, but it does not formalize or argue for the specific hybrid structured–episodic decomposition as a principled organizing abstraction; that higher-level reframing is a novel conceptual contribution relative to this paper.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper contributes explicit conceptual reframings: recasting knowledge-graph generation as graph-difference prediction and treating both graphs and valid-actions as permutation-invariant Sets-of-Sequences to guide architecture and loss design.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent memory itself as a first-class hybrid abstraction combining structured relational stores and episodic/reflective memory with retrieval/composition primitives as core design elements for robust LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's reframings are explicit and central; the higher-level memory-as-hybrid abstraction and the prioritization of selective retrieval/composition as a guiding abstraction are not present in the paper and therefore represent a novel conceptual reframing.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper frames prompted LLMs plus symbolic modules as 'neurosymbolic reasoners' and focuses on prompting, module integration, and constrained action selection as central design elements; it does not reframe agent design around memory architecture.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes LLM-agent design around a memory-centric abstraction: treat memory as a hybrid system (structured relational store + episodic/reflection log) with selective retrieval/composition as the primary design knob determining robustness and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This conceptual reframing—elevating hybrid memory structure and retrieval policy to the principal organizing abstraction for LLM-agent design—is absent from the paper and constitutes a novel abstraction-level contribution.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes agent memory/prompt history as an opportunity for task-agnostic, human-interpretable abstraction: representing histories as relational text deltas rather than repeated full observations.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a higher-level reframing: agent memory should be conceptualized as a hybrid architecture (explicit structured store + episodic/reflection buffer) where retrieval selectivity and compositional integration are first-class design axes, as important as raw capacity or modality.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The delta-as-abstraction reframing is central to the paper, but the broader architectural reframing (persistent hybrid memory with explicit retrieval/composition semantics) is not presented or evaluated in the paper and thus represents a novel conceptual lens.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides a clear conceptual reframing that external symbolic procedures can be treated as first-class actions in an agent's action space (neurosymbolic action-injection), allowing a sequence-model to learn to call tool-like modules via imitation learning; this reframing is explicit and operationalized in the experiments.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent memory architecture as an essential design principle: a hybrid of compact structured representations plus episodic/reflection stores with selective retrieval and composition as the primary abstraction for scalable, generalizable LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's action-as-tools reframing is explicit and contributes a practical abstraction, but the higher-level architectural reframing (structured+episodic memory as the organizing abstraction) is not articulated or evaluated in the paper and therefore constitutes a novel conceptual contribution relative to it.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper frames the problem as large action spaces solved by a generator (LM) producing candidates and an RL re-ranker (DRRN) selecting actions, and discusses related approaches (affordance models, knowledge-graph augmentation) as alternative strategies.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory introduces an explicit abstraction that organizes agent memory into two modalities—structured relational memory and episodic/reflection memory—and elevates selective retrieval and composition as first-class architectural primitives on par with memory capacity or modality.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The CALM paper does not present this memory-modality taxonomy or advocate designing agents by explicitly composing structured and episodic stores; the proposed reframing is a new conceptual lens not embodied in the paper's formulation or experiments.

empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper aggregates empirical patterns and practice-level regularities: summarization/compression and selective retrieval improve long-horizon performance, active maintenance and belief-state carryover improve short-term consistency, and structured memories (graphs/trees) enable multi-hop retrieval and relational reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It synthesizes cross-paper qualitative regularities and best-practice patterns, mapping method families to observed benefits across environments and tasks (summarized in tables and taxonomy mappings).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The meta-regularities are an explicit synthesis of prior empirical findings; they provide useful heuristics but are qualitative and not accompanied by new cross-study statistical meta-analysis or formal validation.

empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper aggregates experiments and ablations across multiple games, NetHack conditions, and QA benchmarks, comparing AriGraph to several LLM-memory baselines and RL baselines and measuring graph growth/update and token costs.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It synthesizes evidence that the hybrid structured+episodic graph memory plus selective retrieval consistently improves agent performance and scales to harder variants, and it reports secondary empirical patterns (graph growth dynamics, ablation outcomes, token trade-offs).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The cross-task empirical regularity — that this hybrid memory architecture yields robust gains across interactive POMDP-like text games and retrieval QA in these experiments — is an explicitly established, experimentally supported meta-regularity.

empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Single-environment demonstrations of retrieval or reflection exist, but there was limited published synthesis showing a single retrieval recipe consistently boosts performance across both text-only and embodied multimodal benchmarks and across multiple LLM/VLM backbones.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper aggregates controlled experiments across four benchmarks, ablation studies over retriever choices, and cross-model memory-transfer tests to demonstrate a repeated pattern: contextually selective retrieval improves success rates and rewards across tasks and modalities.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The multi-benchmark empirical regularity—that the same retrieval-augmented planning design yields sizable, reproducible improvements across diverse agent settings—is established by the reported results.

empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent based on Theory of Mind in Guandan (搜蛋): A Multi-Player Cooperative Game under Imperfect Information

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper aggregates experiments across multiple LLMs, ToM orders, top-k settings, and language environments and consistently shows that ToM prompting plus top-k action filtering improves performance, with K=5 outperforming K=3 and GPT-4 benefiting most.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: An empirical regularity: across tested models and conditions, explicit belief reasoning plus selective action filtering yields consistent gains in the Guandan domain and narrows (but does not fully close) the gap to SOTA RL.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The cross-model, cross-language experimental synthesis provides a reproducible pattern supporting the robustness of these interventions in this domain; it is an empirical meta-regularity specific to the tested setup.

empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper compiles multi-game empirical results showing a consistent pattern: DBERT-augmented DRRN/TDQN outperforms or matches several prior baselines, attains SOTA on 4 of 14 games, and does so with fewer environment interactions in training compared to many baselines.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It synthesizes evidence that LM-derived priors transfer useful semantics across games and that transcript fine-tuning of a compact LM amplifies these benefits, improving both learning stability and sample efficiency on the Jericho benchmark suite.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This multi-game empirical synthesis is new for the specific approach (DistilBERT fine-tuned on gameplay transcripts as a reusable encoder) and provides actionable evidence for the research direction, but it does not establish broader meta-regularities about memory architecture (structured+episodic) beyond the demonstrated LM benefit.

empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games
