aggregated_results.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper aggregates empirical patterns and practice-level regularities: summarization/compression and selective retrieval improve long-horizon performance, active maintenance and belief-state carryover improve short-term consistency, and structured memories (graphs/trees) enable multi-hop retrieval and relational reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It synthesizes cross-paper qualitative regularities and best-practice patterns, mapping method families to observed benefits across environments and tasks (summarized in tables and taxonomy mappings).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The meta-regularities are an explicit synthesis of prior empirical findings; they provide useful heuristics but are qualitative and not accompanied by new cross-study statistical meta-analysis or formal validation.

aggregated_results.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper aggregates experiments and ablations across multiple games, NetHack conditions, and QA benchmarks, comparing AriGraph to several LLM-memory baselines and RL baselines and measuring graph growth/update and token costs.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It synthesizes evidence that the hybrid structured+episodic graph memory plus selective retrieval consistently improves agent performance and scales to harder variants, and it reports secondary empirical patterns (graph growth dynamics, ablation outcomes, token trade-offs).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The cross-task empirical regularity — that this hybrid memory architecture yields robust gains across interactive POMDP-like text games and retrieval QA in these experiments — is an explicitly established, experimentally supported meta-regularity.

aggregated_results.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Single-environment demonstrations of retrieval or reflection exist, but there was limited published synthesis showing a single retrieval recipe consistently boosts performance across both text-only and embodied multimodal benchmarks and across multiple LLM/VLM backbones.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper aggregates controlled experiments across four benchmarks, ablation studies over retriever choices, and cross-model memory-transfer tests to demonstrate a repeated pattern: contextually selective retrieval improves success rates and rewards across tasks and modalities.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The multi-benchmark empirical regularity—that the same retrieval-augmented planning design yields sizable, reproducible improvements across diverse agent settings—is established by the reported results.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent based on Theory of Mind in Guandan (搜蛋): A Multi-Player Cooperative Game under Imperfect Information

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper aggregates experiments across multiple LLMs, ToM orders, top-k settings, and language environments and consistently shows that ToM prompting plus top-k action filtering improves performance, with K=5 outperforming K=3 and GPT-4 benefiting most.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: An empirical regularity: across tested models and conditions, explicit belief reasoning plus selective action filtering yields consistent gains in the Guandan domain and narrows (but does not fully close) the gap to SOTA RL.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The cross-model, cross-language experimental synthesis provides a reproducible pattern supporting the robustness of these interventions in this domain; it is an empirical meta-regularity specific to the tested setup.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper compiles multi-game empirical results showing a consistent pattern: DBERT-augmented DRRN/TDQN outperforms or matches several prior baselines, attains SOTA on 4 of 14 games, and does so with fewer environment interactions in training compared to many baselines.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It synthesizes evidence that LM-derived priors transfer useful semantics across games and that transcript fine-tuning of a compact LM amplifies these benefits, improving both learning stability and sample efficiency on the Jericho benchmark suite.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This multi-game empirical synthesis is new for the specific approach (DistilBERT fine-tuned on gameplay transcripts as a reusable encoder) and provides actionable evidence for the research direction, but it does not establish broader meta-regularities about memory architecture (structured+episodic) beyond the demonstrated LM benefit.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper synthesizes empirical results across 10 tasks and four architectures, showing consistent patterns: affordance injection (particularly via KG) improves performance in the majority of cases (34/40 variants), MCA helps less consistently, LMs benefited especially from affordances, and the method of injection matters (KG > separate encoder > concat).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a meta-regularity: robust performance and generalization in LLM agents require principled integration of structured and episodic memory modalities with selective retrieval/composition, i.e., a universal empirical pattern across complex interactive-agent settings.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's cross-task/model synthesis supports parts of this meta-regularity (structured affordances and selective memory design matter) but does not claim or validate a universal law; the theory's global meta-claim is an extrapolation beyond the paper's empirical scope and is therefore a derivable but unstated synthesis.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides an empirical synthesis within the IF literature: across 28 varied games, the combination of a knowledge graph, template actions, graph masking, and valid-action supervision yields a repeatable pattern of improved exploration and competitive or superior scores relative to baselines.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory-level meta-regularity claimed is broader: it asserts that the same pattern (necessity of structured representations plus selective retrieval) will hold across LLM-based agents and complex partially observable tasks, and that additionally integration with episodic/reflection memory is required for robust generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper establishes a robust empirical regularity within IF games, extrapolating this to a cross-domain meta-regularity that explicitly requires hybrid structured+episodic memory is an extrapolation beyond the presented experiments and constitutes a new synthesized claim.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Empirically, the paper reports a cross-domain regularity: distilled verbal self-reflections reliably improve few-shot trial-and-error performance for capable LLMs across multiple benchmark families, and ablations show distilled reflections outperform raw trajectory inclusion in their settings.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a stronger meta-regularity: across LLM agent settings with partial observability and long horizons, the reliable pattern is that only hybrid structured+episodic memory with selective retrieval/composition yields robust generalization and scaling—mere memory volume or unstructured episodic retention will not reproduce these gains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides multi-task empirical evidence for the value of distilled episodic reflections but lacks cross-paper synthesis or experiments involving structured-memory modalities; the theory's broader meta-regularity (necessity of hybrid memory across settings) is not supported by the paper and therefore remains a novel, higher-level synthesis.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper establishes an empirical regularity within its experiments: Sweet&Sour (positive+negative reflection + managed memory) consistently improves average success on ScienceWorld across model sizes, with a larger relative benefit for smaller LLMs and a measurable drop when only failures are used.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader meta-regularity: across tasks/environments the dominant determinant of scalable, generalizable performance is memory structure/selectivity/retrieval (and hybridizing structured and episodic memory), rather than raw memory capacity or mere persistence of history.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper supplies a single-environment empirical pattern that supports managed episodic memory, but it does not synthesize multi-environment evidence or establish the broader cross-domain meta-regularity that structure/selectivity dominate memory benefits.

aggregated_results.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides multi-task empirical evidence within dialogue domains that structured attribute augmentations and prioritized selective retrieval produce consistent improvements in retrieval recall and downstream task metrics across datasets and LLM backbones.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader meta-regularity: across complex partially-observable, long-horizon agent domains, the fundamental regularity is that hybrid structured–episodic memory plus selective retrieval/composition is necessary for robust generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper establishes a narrower cross-task regularity within dialogue tasks but does not synthesize evidence across agent/text-game literature nor demonstrate necessity in long-horizon planning settings; the broader meta-regularity claimed by the theory is therefore not established here and would be an extrapolation beyond the paper's results.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Empirically, the paper provides cross-game evidence within textual environments that structured KG representations, graph-diff prediction, multi-task learning, and an SOS loss produce consistent gains on KG and valid-action prediction metrics across multiple unseen games and ablations.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a meta-regularity across studies and domains: that memory structure, selectivity, and retrieval/composition matter at least as much as raw memory capacity or modality for robust, generalizable LLM-based agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper supplies in-domain empirical support for the value of structured memory, but it does not synthesize cross-study or cross-domain evidence to establish the broader meta-regularity; the cross-study/meta claim is therefore not present and is novel relative to this work.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Within its experimental regime, the paper establishes an empirical regularity: learned graph-structured belief representations consistently improve test performance and generalization across hundreds of TextWorld games and difficulty levels, and ground-truth graphs further improve results.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader meta-regularity across LLM-based agents and tasks: that hybrid structured-plus-episodic memory and selective retrieval/composition are regular, necessary ingredients for robust performance in complex, partially observable language-based environments.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides a single-study empirical pattern limited to TextWorld; it does not synthesize evidence across multiple independent domains, scales, or LLM modalities to establish the wider meta-regularity the theory claims, so that synthesis is novel relative to the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides cross-task empirical results showing strong average performance for prompted LLM agents with symbolic modules and documents task-specific memory failures, but it does not synthesize these observations into a general cross-task regularity about memory representations.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts an empirical meta-regularity: across complex, partially observable, text-game-like tasks, structured relational memory combined with episodic/reflection mechanisms and selective retrieval consistently determine scalable, robust performance (more so than raw memory size or single-modality storage).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's experiments are suggestive but do not perform the cross-task synthesis or statistical analysis required to establish this meta-regularity; thus the claimed synthesis is not present in the paper and must be treated as a novel higher-level inference.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper synthesizes empirical evidence within its single study: controlled ablations quantitatively show the full architecture outperforms ablations on believability (TrueSkill/ranks), and the two-day simulation demonstrates concrete emergent phenomena (diffusion, relationship formation, coordination) when the full architecture is used. The authors corroborate retrieval/ reflection/planning contributions qualitatively and quantitatively.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory elevates this to a general empirical regularity claim across tasks and domains: that selective structured retrieval combined with episodic/reflection memory will be necessary for robust performance of LLM agents in complex environments, not just an artifact of this sandbox.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides strong in-domain empirical synthesis but does not aggregate evidence across independent domains or long timescales to establish the cross-domain meta-regularity; therefore the broader empirical-regularity claim remains a plausible inference rather than a demonstrated universal pattern.

aggregated_results.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Empirically, the paper synthesizes regularities within its experiments: diff histories produce denser learning signals, increase sample efficiency, can compress tokens in high-dimensional settings, and scale with additional labeled data, with environment-dependent effects.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a meta-regularity across tasks and architectures: structured memory combined with episodic/reflection mechanisms and selective retrieval produces synergistic gains that are necessary for robust generalization and scaling of LLM agents in complex POMDP-like domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides a limited empirical synthesis across two environments and related ablations, but it does not aggregate evidence across multiple memory architectures or validate hybrid designs; the broader meta-regularity (hybrid-memory synergy as a general law) is an extrapolation beyond the paper's synthesis.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper synthesizes empirical results across four distinct benchmark types and demonstrates a consistent meta-regularity: behavior-cloned transformers trained with gold trajectories reliably exploit injected symbolic modules to produce large performance and efficiency gains, whereas the tested DRRN reinforcement baseline does not benefit under the same conditions.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader meta-regularity that robust, generalizable performance across tasks depends on integrating structured memory and episodic/reflection memory with selective retrieval; naive memory modalities alone will not scale.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's synthesis supports the importance of structured external tools for the specific benchmarks and agent regimen, but it does not synthesize evidence about episodic/reflection memory or selective retrieval across tasks or literature; the broader meta-regularity claimed by the theory is not established by this paper and remains a novel higher-level synthesis.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper synthesizes empirical results across 28 games showing consistent patterns: LM priors improve candidate quality, pretraining + human transcripts matter, and top-k controls a precision/recall exploration-exploitation tradeoff; several games exhibit where CALM outperforms handicap-assisted agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a higher-level empirical regularity: consistent robust generalization across complex text tasks arises only when agents employ both structured relational representations and episodic/reflection memory with selective retrieval, i.e., a cross-method common factor driving success.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper reports reproducible within-experiment regularities for LM generation and k/pretraining effects, it does not synthesize evidence across methods to establish that hybrid memory is the common causal factor; that meta-regularity is an inference beyond the presented empirical synthesis.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior empirical findings showed benefits of KGs or structured memory for RL agents and separate works showed LLM reflection/LLM priors can aid planning in simpler settings, but no prior single study reported cross-game, ablated evidence of memory-guided LLM priors inside MCTS on hard Jericho games.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper provides a multi-game empirical synthesis (9 Jericho games) plus ablations demonstrating a consistent pattern: adding bounded cross-trial reflections and short in-trial context to an LLM prior inside MCTS yields robust initial-planning improvements and alleviates bottleneck failures compared to LLM-only, reflection-only, PUCT-RL and MC-LAVE-RL baselines.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The cross-game empirical regularity that selective, bounded episodic reflections combined with short in-trial context systematically improves initial MCTS planning on challenging text-game benchmarks is newly documented and supported by ablations.

aggregated_results.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides empirical evidence from a single line of experiments that a KG-augmented MoE (frozen experts + hot expert + attention) improves zero/few-shot performance in their specific LIGHT-based open-world tasks and explores robustness to distractors and absent experts.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a cross-system empirical regularity: across LLM-based agents and complex partially-observable tasks, structured memory plus selective episodic retrieval is generally required for robust performance, compression, and compositional reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's single-domain empirical results do not constitute a multi-study synthesis across agent classes or environments; the broad meta-regularity claimed by the theory is not established by the paper and represents a new, higher-level empirical claim.

generalization_scope_expansion.what_is_known: A comprehensive survey explicitly documents memory structure (graphs, attributes), selective retrieval (semantic similarity, filtering), and compositional mechanisms as effective design principles across six game genres, with validated special-case caveats (trivial tasks, random environments). Additionally, 14 papers demonstrate these techniques' benefits in specific domains: interactive fiction (GATA, CALM, KG-A2C, Worldformer), embodied/multimodal tasks (RAP), dialogue (MemInsight), role-playing (Thespian, Generative Agents), planning (Reflexion, AriGraph), and cooperative games (Guandan, Sweet&Sour).
generalization_scope_expansion.what_introduced: The theory statement elevates these empirically-validated design principles to a fundamental architectural necessity claim: that memory augmentation benefits depend not just on presence or volume but **crucially** on structure, access patterns, and composition; and that unstructured brute-force or naive concatenation **fails to scale** in complex games. This frames structure/selectivity/composition as **co-equal in criticality** to memory size or modality—a stronger necessity claim than 'effective technique'—and positions it as a domain-general requirement across all nontrivial partially-observable, long-horizon LLM agent settings.
generalization_scope_expansion.what_novel: The elevation from 'effective and broadly-applicable design principle' (established in survey) to 'fundamental architectural necessity that is as critical as memory size itself and whose absence causes scaling failure' represents a scope and strength expansion. While the survey and multiple papers establish that these techniques work well across domains, the specific claim that they are **necessary** (not just beneficial), **co-equal** to memory size, and that naive approaches **fail to scale** is stated more strongly than established literature. This represents an explicit but peripheral claim—the core insight is documented, but the necessity framing and co-equal importance assertion go beyond established statements.

One survey paper (LLM Game Agents Survey) compiles these techniques as broadly applicable design principles across game genres with documented special-case caveats (trivial tasks, random environments).

This positions structure/selectivity/composition as **as critical as** memory size or modality, framing it as a domain-general requirement for robust performance across any nontrivial partially-observable, long-horizon LLM agent setting.

The statement is derivable by aggregating narrow results but has not been articulated as established domain-general knowledge.

theory_law_dict.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
theory_law_dict.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.
theory_law_dict.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
theory_law_dict.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
theory_law_dict.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

aggregated_results.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The survey generalizes memory and reasoning design principles across six game genres and across modalities (textual, symbolic, visual), arguing for which memory design choices address which genre-specific demands (e.g., low-latency vs long-horizon).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It explicitly extends and recommends memory-structuring and selective retrieval approaches as broadly relevant across nontrivial, partially observable, long-horizon game settings, and it documents special-case caveats (trivial small tasks, fully random environments).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This is a literature-level generalization presented by the authors; it compiles and projects known techniques to broader settings but does not introduce a new mathematical generalization or proof.

aggregated_results.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: RAG and memory-augmented techniques have been primarily evaluated on knowledge-intensive NLP tasks or limited textual agent tasks; explicit application to embodied multimodal agents with visual observations and policy mapping has been comparatively under-explored.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: RAP extends retrieval-augmented episodic memory methods to multimodal embodied benchmarks (visual observations represented via CLIP/VLMs), demonstrates memory transfer across model pairs, and shows improvements across both text and visual-agent domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The empirical extension of retrieval-augmented episodic memory to multimodal embodied decision-making and the demonstration of cross-model memory reuse materially broadens the domain of these techniques and is validated in the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Knowledge-graph methods have been applied primarily to static QA/document tasks and episodic approaches to isolated agent reflections; prior LLM-agent memory work often evaluated on narrower environment sets or single memory modalities.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper demonstrates AriGraph across diverse domains (several TextWorld tasks of increasing difficulty, NetHack partial-observation experiments, and multi-hop QA), arguing the approach generalizes beyond static QA to interactive, partially observable, long-horizon tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This constitutes an empirical scope expansion showing applicability of hybrid KG+episodic memory to interactive POMDP-style text games and to retrieval Q&A, though the claim is demonstrated experimentally rather than proven theoretically.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent based on Theory of Mind in Guandan (搜蛋): A Multi-Player Cooperative Game under Imperfect Information

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work has shown ToM prompting and tool use can help LLM agents in simpler or two-player settings (mostly English), and that filtering/candidate selection can aid decision systems in other domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: An explicit empirical generalization: ToM-based planning combined with an external action-recommender improves LLM agent performance in a harder, four-player cooperative imperfect-information Chinese card game (Guandan) across multiple LLM families and prompt orders.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The contribution is an applied scope expansion demonstrating these interventions transfer to a non-English, multi-player, long-horizon environment; it is an empirical extension rather than a novel theoretical generalization.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The authors fine-tune DistilBERT on a large set of human gameplay transcripts (ClubFloyd) distinct from evaluation games and evaluate the same LM-augmented agents across 14 diverse Jericho games of varying difficulty, reporting cross-game performance and a limited cross-game transfer test (Zork1→Zork3).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper provides evidence that a single LM instance, adapted to game language via transcript fine-tuning, generalizes across multiple human-written IF games and reduces required environment interactions compared to several baselines.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper explicitly demonstrates across-game generalization within the IF/Jericho domain, it does not claim or show that this generalization extends beyond similar IF-style, text-adventure environments or to modalities beyond text; the scope expansion is empirical and domain-limited.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Experiments are limited to one newly designed non-cooperative game (Goedendag) at two board scales (4x4 and 10x10); authors mention possible extensions (cooperative games, memory bank) but provide no cross-domain empirical validation.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory claims broad scope: that hybrid structured–episodic memory and selective retrieval are necessary for robust scaling and generalization of LLM agents across partially observable, long-horizon, branching text-game domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides limited empirical support for generalization and only suggests memory-bank extensions; the broad, cross-domain generalization claim of the theory is not demonstrated and thus remains an unproven extrapolation (derivable in principle but unstated and unvalidated here).

aggregated_results.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The experiments and claims in the paper are scoped to text role-playing/adventure games in the LIGHT environment and to models built on KG-A2C-style architectures; results show benefits in these settings (character disentanglement, few-shot blending).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory extends the claim to a much broader scope: that memory structure, selectivity, and retrieval/composition are as critical as memory size or modality for any LLM-based agent operating in nontrivial partially-observable, long/branching-horizon domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the task-specific findings to a domain-general necessity statement is not established by this paper; the paper's results make the generalization plausible but do not provide cross-domain evidence or theoretical proof.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper's empirical claims are specific to the ALFWorld benchmark and GPT2-medium models with up to a 1000-token context; it briefly speculates about few-shot priming and broader trends but offers no empirical or theoretical claims that generalize to arbitrary long-horizon, highly-branching text-game domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory extends the claim to a broad scope: for any LLM-based agent operating in nontrivial state/action spaces with partial observability and long or branching horizons, hybrid structured–episodic memory and selective retrieval are required for robust scaling and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That broad, cross-environment scope is not argued or demonstrated by the paper; while the paper's context-window limits make such issues plausible, the necessity claim across domains is not established and must be inferred beyond the paper's results.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates that distilled verbal self-reflection generalizes across multiple task families (ALFWorld, HotPotQA, HumanEval/MBPP/Leetcode) and notes practical limits (small bounded mem windows, context-size constraints) and potential future memory backends (vector DBs, SQL).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory expands the scope by claiming the Reflexion-style benefits should extend only as part of a hybrid structured+episodic memory architecture with selective retrieval/composition, and that this hybrid is necessary across complex, partially observable, long-horizon domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending Reflexion’s episodic-memory result to a required hybrid structured+episodic architecture is an extrapolation not empirically tested in the paper; the hybrid-as-necessary claim is a scope expansion derivable from but not demonstrated by the paper's experiments or analyses.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates benefits of retrieval plus reflection/verification in the Jubensha narrative multi-agent domain, including experiments with full-script access and ablations across module combinations and LLM capabilities (GPT-3.5 vs GPT-4).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes this requirement to all LLM-based agents operating in nontrivial state/action spaces with partial observability and long/branching planning horizons, claiming that hybrid structured-episodic memory and selective retrieval are necessary for scaling and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides domain-specific empirical evidence but does not test across diverse environments, modalities, or structured-memory implementations; the broad cross-domain generalization is asserted by the theory but not demonstrated or derived in the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reports results from a single, comprehensive environment (ScienceWorld) and states Sweet&Sour is broadly applicable in principle, but it does not provide multi-environment empirical validation or formal generalization guarantees.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory expands the claim to a domain-wide requirement: across complex, partially observable, long- or branching-horizon text-game settings, memory structure, selectivity, and retrieval/composition (including hybrid structured-episodic memory) are essential for scaling and generalization beyond mere memory volume or naive history retention.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the paper's single-environment managed-episodic finding to a domain-wide necessary condition for hybrid structured-episodic memory is not established by the paper; the broader scope claim goes beyond the presented experiments and is not explicitly derived there.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper shows that the Worldformer components (graph-diff prediction, multi-tasking, SOS loss) generalize across multiple unseen text games within the domain of interactive fiction, indicating improved zero-shot transfer among similar textual environments.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes beyond the paper by claiming that hybrid structured–episodic memory and principled selective retrieval are required for robust performance and scaling of LLM-based agents in any nontrivial partially observable, long-horizon domain.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the paper's in-domain empirical findings to a broad claim about all LLM-based agents and domains is not supported by the experiments; this broader scope expansion is not established here and is a novel generalization.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates the architecture only in one engineered domain (the Smallville sandbox) with 25 agents over two simulated days, and while the authors argue the primitives are likely to generalize as models improve, they explicitly acknowledge limited timescale, domain, and cost constraints.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory extends the claim to a broad scope: that hybrid structured-episodic memory with selective retrieval is a general requirement for robust performance across LLM-based agents operating in any nontrivial state/action space with partial observability and long/branching horizons.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That cross-domain, broadly-scoped requirement is not empirically demonstrated in the paper; it is a plausible and natural generalization from the paper's results but remains an unproven extrapolation beyond the single-domain experiments.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior LLM-planning results were typically shown on simpler or more structured tasks; methods that scale RL or KG-based representations to complex Jericho-style games required iterative learning/large training budgets.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper extends LLM-guided planning to long-horizon, partially observable, high-branching text games (Jericho suite) and shows that memory-guided LLM priors produce strong initial-planning performance without multi-iteration planning-then-learning cycles; it also documents implementation choices (memory sizes, truncation, dynamic pruning) needed to operate within prompt-size limits.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Empirical scope expansion to hard, long-horizon text games with bounded reflection memory and dynamic pruning is new in practice, demonstrating that these design choices enable LLM+MCTS to be effective in this domain.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper evaluates action-injection across four parametrically varied benchmark families in partially observable, multi-step text-game settings and shows reproducible improvements for the behavior-cloned transformer; it also notes settings where module actions increase training cost or action-space size and acknowledges trivial cases where modules add little value.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes to a broad claim that in any nontrivial state/action-space, partially observable, long-horizon domain, robust generalization requires a hybrid of structured and episodic memory with selective retrieval and composition rather than mere memory volume or naive concatenation.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides task-limited empirical support that structured external tools help sequence models in text games, but it neither evaluates episodic/reflection memory mechanisms nor demonstrates the proposed hybrid requirement across domains; the broader scope is plausible but not established by the paper and would require additional theoretical/empirical work to substantiate.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper's experiments and claims are explicitly scoped to ScienceWorld (10 elementary-school science tasks) and four agent types (DRRN, KG-A2C, RoBERTa, Swift) with specific injection strategies and ablations; results are reported per task and per model.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes from these results to a broad domain claim: that hybrid structured-episodic memory with selective retrieval is a fundamental requirement for robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility for LLM-based agents in complex, partially observable, long-horizon/text-game-like settings (and implicitly beyond).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This domain-level generalization (elevating empirical findings from one environment and limited agent classes to a general requirement across LLM-agent settings) is not established by the paper and represents a substantive scope expansion beyond its evidence.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates that the KG + template + graph-mask + valid-action supervision approach generalizes across a diverse set of 28 human-authored IF games, arguing broad applicability within the interactive-fiction domain and across different game genres and structures.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes this result far beyond IF: it claims that for any LLM-based agent operating in nontrivial, partially observable, long- or branching-horizon environments, hybrid structured-episodic memory with selective retrieval is a fundamental requirement for robust generalization and strategic flexibility.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That domain-agnostic, task-agnostic general requirement (moving from IF-game evidence to a general law for LLM agents across modalities and training regimes) is not supported or tested by the paper and therefore represents a substantive scope expansion.

aggregated_results.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Experiments show that MemInsight's augmentation + selective retrieval generalizes across three dialogue-centered tasks and multiple LLM backbones within multi-session conversational datasets (LLM-REDIAL, LoCoMo).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory extends the claim beyond dialogues: it asserts that hybrid structured–episodic memory is a fundamental requirement for robust performance in LLM-based agents operating in complex, partially observable, long-horizon, branching text-game environments.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending necessity claims to long-horizon, partially observable planning/text-game settings goes beyond the empirical domain tested in the paper (dialogue datasets) and is not supported or evaluated there, so this scope expansion is novel relative to the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates generalization empirically within TextWorld: a single GATA policy trained across many sampled games generalizes better to unseen games and difficulty mixes than text-only baselines, and ground-truth graphs raise the upper bound.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory extends the claim to a much broader scope: that hybrid structured-episodic memory and selective retrieval are fundamental requirements for robust performance of LLM-based agents across nontrivial partially observable, long-horizon domains (beyond TextWorld).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides domain-specific evidence (text games) but does not empirically or theoretically establish the cross-domain/LLM-scale generalization or the claim of fundamental necessity; extending the claim beyond the paper's experimental regime is novel relative to this work.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper's empirical scope is limited to four symbolic text-game benchmarks (Arithmetic, MapReader, Sorting, TWC) and primarily evaluates prompt+module integration; it only briefly suggests extending to more complex domains without formal claims about general architectural requirements.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes to a broader domain: for LLM-based agents operating in any nontrivial state/action space with partial observability and long/branching planning horizons, robust generalization requires hybrid structured-episodic memory plus selective retrieval rather than mere increases in context window or raw episodic logs.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This broad scope-expansion claim is not argued or demonstrated by the paper's experiments and therefore represents a genuinely new generalization beyond the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates diff history benefits across two procedurally generated text environments (BabyAI-Text and NetHack) and characterizes when diff helps or hurts (structured observations vs. narrative/unstructured text).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory expands the scope to assert that for all LLM-based agents operating in nontrivial state/action POMDP-like settings with long or branching horizons, success depends on hybrid structured-episodic memory and selective retrieval/composition rather than merely increasing memory size or sticking to one modality.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper provides cross-environment supportive evidence for structured, selective representations, it does not empirically or analytically establish the universal scope claimed by the theory; the global scope expansion is an extrapolation beyond the tested settings.

aggregated_results.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper's scope and experiments are limited to KG-A2C agents in a LIGHT-based open-ended text-roleplaying environment and to mixtures of frozen role-specific experts plus one trainable expert; it does not evaluate large pretrained LLMs, multimodal agents, or broad classes of partially-observable tasks beyond their environment.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes the claim to all LLM-based agents operating in partially observable, long-horizon, branching domains, stating that hybrid structured–episodic memory with selective retrieval is a fundamental requirement for robust scaling and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the paper's specific KG+MoE result to a general requirement for all LLM-based agents and tasks is not supported by the experiments or analysis in the paper and therefore constitutes a substantive scope expansion beyond the paper's evidence.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates cross-game generalization of a single CALM model trained on diverse human transcripts to unseen Jericho games, and studies robustness across 28 man-made games and several decoding/ablation settings (k, pretraining, dataset subsets).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes beyond those experiments to claim an architectural requirement for all LLM-based agents in partially observable, long-horizon, branching text environments: that scaling and generalization depend more on memory structure and selective retrieval than on raw memory size or unstructured modality.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's empirical generalization (single LM generalizing across games) is a narrower result; the stronger claim that hybrid structured-episodic memory is broadly necessary across domains and scales is not supported or tested by the paper and therefore represents a scope expansion beyond the presented evidence.

empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_is_known: At least 10 papers explicitly establish that memory structure (graphs, attributes, deltas), selective retrieval mechanisms (embedding-based search, attribute filtering, graph traversal), and principled composition materially improve LLM agent performance in text-based environments compared to unstructured or naive concatenation approaches. The LLMGA survey explicitly documents recurring failure modes (context overload, temporal inconsistency, capacity degradation) across multiple studies. Individual empirical demonstrations span knowledge-graph agents (AriGraph, GATA), memory systems (MemInsight, Generative Agents, Reflexion), and structured representations (Graph Constrained RL, Behavior Cloned Transformers, diff History).
empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_introduced: The theory statement synthesizes these distributed empirical findings into a domain-general meta-regularity asserting that memory structure, access patterns, and composition mechanisms are **as critical as** raw memory size or modality for robust agent performance. It frames the necessity of principled memory architecture (specifically hybrid structured-episodic integration with selective retrieval) as a fundamental requirement for scaling to complex, partially observable, long-horizon tasks, explicitly predicting that unstructured brute-force approaches fail to scale regardless of capacity.
empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_novel: The component empirical effects (structure helps, selectivity improves performance, naive approaches fail) are explicit and established across the literature. The novel contribution is the **cross-paper synthesis** elevating these into a domain-general comparative principle: that architectural choices (how memory is organized and accessed) are equally or more important than capacity choices (how much is stored or which modality). This comparative, domain-general formulation as a unified meta-regularity—and the specific claim that hybrid structured-episodic integration is necessary for robustness—is not explicitly stated in any surveyed paper but clearly follows from aggregating the documented evidence.

empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_is_known: At least 10 papers explicitly establish that memory structure, selectivity, and retrieval mechanisms materially affect LLM agent performance in text-based environments: structured representations (graphs, attributes, deltas) consistently outperform naive concatenation or unstructured history retention; selective retrieval (embedding-based, attribute-filtering, graph-traversal) reduces noise and improves decision quality; and information overload from brute-force context window usage is a documented, recurring failure mode. These individual empirical effects are well-documented across surveys (LLMGA survey), knowledge-graph agents (AriGraph, GATA, Graph Constrained RL), memory systems (MemInsight, Generative Agents), and structured action spaces (Behavior Cloned Transformers, Knowledge-enhanced Agents).
empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_introduced: The theory statement synthesizes these distributed findings into a unified meta-regularity: that memory structure, access patterns, and composition mechanisms are as critical as raw memory size or modality for robust LLM agent performance in complex, partially observable, long-horizon tasks. It explicitly frames the necessity of principled integration (hybrid structured-episodic memory with selective retrieval) as a domain-general requirement rather than task-specific engineering. The formulation elevates scattered empirical observations into a named, falsifiable principle that unstructured brute-force approaches fundamentally fail to scale.
empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_novel: The core empirical components (structure matters, selectivity helps, naive concatenation fails) are explicit and established across multiple papers. However, the specific synthesis—articulating these as a unified meta-regularity with equal or greater importance than memory volume/modality, and framing hybrid structured-episodic integration as a fundamental architectural requirement—is not explicitly stated as a general principle in any surveyed paper. Seven papers provide no evidence for this integration requirement (genuinely new relative to them), four papers contain derivable-but-unstated implications, and two treat related ideas peripherally. The meta-level synthesis represents a derivable-but-previously-unstated regularity across the literature.
