theory_law_dict.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
theory_law_dict.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.
theory_law_dict.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
theory_law_dict.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
theory_law_dict.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically shows that a four-agent, tree-of-thoughts pipeline (summarization → area selection → action selection → validation) yields higher win rates and lower rule-violation error than direct or CoT queries on the evaluated non-cooperative game (Goedendag) across tested board sizes.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It documents the empirical regularity that structuring prompts into task-specific agents and selecting a bounded area of the state-space produces measurable performance and error-rate improvements in this game domain.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Within the scope of the experiments, the effect (task-decomposed agents + selective area → improved win rate and lower error) is an explicit, established empirical finding of the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper collects and documents recurring empirical phenomena reported across LLMGA studies: context overload from naive concatenation, short-term temporal inconsistency (e.g., PokéLLMon switch behaviour), degradation when working memory exceeds effective capacity, and benefit from compression/summary and selective retrieval. These behaviors are illustrated with examples and referenced empirical results from prior work.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The survey synthesizes and names these failure modes and regularities across multiple papers and environments, presenting them as central design challenges for LLMGAs rather than isolated observations.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: No genuinely new empirical phenomenon is reported; the contribution is a consolidated, cross-paper articulation of known failure modes rather than discovery of previously unreported effects.

aggregated_results.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that an LLM agent using an integrated semantic knowledge graph plus linked episodic memory (AriGraph) attains substantially better task performance than agents using unstructured full history, summarization, RAG, Reflexion, or Simulacra across multiple text-game environments and competitive results on multi-hop QA.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: AriGraph shows the empirical regularity that explicitly combining semantic graph structure with episodic vertices and a two-stage retrieval (semantic → episodic) produces consistent, large gains in partially observable, long-horizon text environments and improves retrieval for multi-hop QA.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The empirical effect — that a hybrid structured+episodic graph memory with selective graph-based retrieval materially outperforms common unstructured or single-modality memory schemes in these benchmarks — is presented and validated as a central contribution.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work (KG-A2C, GATA, LIGHT agents) shows structured external memory (knowledge graphs) and prompt-like conditioning improve performance in text games. The paper empirically demonstrates that a single model can be trained to produce distinct per-character behaviors when given learned soft character prompts and per-character action-logit outputs. It also shows that a frozen core plus an attention module can compose pre-trained per-character logits to enable fast few-shot learning of a blended new character.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper introduces the empirical phenomenon that (1) learning soft character prompts together with per-character logit heads allows one model to emulate multiple distinct characters without behavioral entanglement, and (2) an attention-based fusion over raw action logits enables few-shot creation of a new blended character while preserving prior behaviors.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: These specific empirical outcomes (prompt-conditioned per-character logits preventing entanglement and logits-level attention enabling efficient few-shot blending) are contributions of this paper and are presented with experimental evidence in LIGHT environments.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that injecting affordance knowledge (sourced from ConceptNet and integrated as KG triples or via pretraining) improves action selection and cumulative reward across many ScienceWorld tasks, and that preserving a memory of previous correct actions (MCA) yields smaller, task-dependent gains. It also shows that naïve concatenation of affordance strings or adding affordances as raw textual input often harms performance (information overload).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory emphasizes a general phenomenon: that memory augmentation benefits depend on memory structure, selective access, and composition rather than mere volume or modality, and that unstructured brute-force context retention fails to scale in complex text-game agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The core empirical phenomenon (structured/selective memory matters more than raw/contextual volume; KG-based affordances outperform plain concatenation; MCA helps but less consistently) is explicitly established by the paper's experiments and ablations.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that a dynamically constructed knowledge graph plus a template-based action space (with a graph-constrained decoder and valid-action supervision) improves learning speed and final performance across a broad suite of 28 interactive-fiction games; ablations show graph masking/attention speed convergence and that unconstrained, word-level decoding performs poorly. These results establish that structure and selectivity in state and action representations materially affect agent performance in large natural-language action spaces.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory-level phenomenon asserts more broadly that robust performance in LLM-based text-game agents requires a hybrid of structured (relational) and episodic/reflection memory modalities and principled selective retrieval/composition, not merely larger memory capacity or unstructured history retention.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Relative to this paper, the novel empirical claim is the explicit requirement for integration of structured relational memory with episodic/reflective memory and selective composition; the paper documents the importance of structured representations and selectivity but does not provide evidence for the necessity or benefit of an episodic/reflection memory modality.

aggregated_results.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that autonomously generated structured augmentations (attribute mining, turn/session granularity, priority ordering) combined with selective retrieval (attribute-filtering or embedding-based search) improves downstream performance (QA, conversational recommendation, event summarization) and retrieval recall relative to baselines that use all history or unstructured memory.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: MemInsight explicitly documents the empirical effect that structured, attribute-centric memory augmentations plus selective retrieval reduce noise/volume of retrieved context and yield consistent gains across multiple dialogue tasks and backbone models.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The core phenomenon — that memory structure and selective retrieval materially affect LLM-agent performance in multi-session dialogue tasks — is directly established and evaluated in the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that learned, dynamic graph-structured belief states (GATA) improve policy learning and generalization across a distribution of TextWorld games relative to text-only baselines, and that access to full ground-truth graphs yields a higher performance upper bound. It also reports that discrete updater variants can be brittle and that continuous belief graphs encode actionable relational information for decision making.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The evaluated theory claims a broader phenomenon: robust performance in LLM-based text-game agents requires a principled hybrid of structured (relational/graph) memory and episodic/reflection-style memory, with structure, selectivity, and retrieval quality as important as raw memory volume or modality.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper establishes the empirical phenomenon that structured graph memory helps in TextWorld, but it does not establish the stronger, domain-general phenomenon that a hybrid structured+episodic memory is a fundamental, necessary requirement for robust performance across LLM agents and long-horizon partially observable tasks.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that augmenting LLM-driven agents with a persistent natural-language memory stream plus selective retrieval, periodic reflection (recursive synthesis), and planning produces markedly more believable individual behavior and emergent social dynamics (information diffusion, relationship formation, coordination) than ablated architectures. Ablation experiments quantify degraded believability when observation, reflection, or planning are removed. The end-to-end two-day multi-agent simulation shows these components produce concrete emergent phenomena in the Smallville sandbox.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory frames a named, broader phenomenon: that robust performance in LLM-based text-game agents requires a principled integration of structured (relational/graph-like) memory and episodic/reflection memory, with selective retrieval and composition being critical beyond mere memory volume or naive context concatenation.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The core empirical phenomenon the theory asserts—selective, structured retrieval combined with episodic/reflection leading to improved agent behavior—is directly demonstrated and measured in the paper’s sandbox and ablation studies, so the phenomenon is present and empirically supported within the paper's domain.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically shows that injecting symbolic-module actions (arithmetic, navigation, sorting, KB lookup) into the action space yields large, consistent performance and efficiency gains for a behavior-cloned transformer across four text-game benchmarks, raising average task performance to near-ceiling while leaving the tested RL baseline (DRRN) unimproved. The experiments used parametric train/dev/test splits and report aggregated results over 100 unseen games per benchmark. The paper also documents the action-space expansion caused by some modules (e.g., ~530 KB queries).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It introduces and names the practical phenomenon that treating external symbolic tools as first-class actions (action-space injection) enables a sequence-model (behavior cloning with a pretrained transformer) to reliably exploit those tools and substantially improve multi-step interactive task performance and efficiency.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This empirical effect is explicitly demonstrated and established by the paper for the tested domains and agent setup, but it does not assert or test any claims about episodic or reflection memory or the need to integrate structured and episodic memory modalities.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that adding a vector-based Memory Retriever (embedding store + Faiss top-k retrieval) and complementary modules (Self-Refinement, Self-Verification) improves agents' factual and inferential accuracy, increases similarity between chat histories and full scripts, and raises murderer-identification accuracy and civilian win rate in the Jubensha multi-agent text-game setting.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a broader phenomenon: that not merely memory volume or modality but the internal structure of memory, selective retrieval strategies, and how retrieved content is composed with ongoing reasoning are critical determinants of agent performance and scalability; naive unstructured concatenation leads to overload or sparsity and fails on complex, long-horizon tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already documents the empirical effect that semantic retrieval plus reflection/verification improves performance in this domain, but it does not empirically or conceptually establish the stronger, domain-general phenomenon that structured (e.g., graph) + episodic hybrid memory and principled composition are a fundamental requirement across tasks.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent based on Theory of Mind in Guandan (搜蛋): A Multi-Player Cooperative Game under Imperfect Information

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically documents that off-the-shelf LLM agents underperform a SOTA RL agent (Danzero+) in the multi-player imperfect-information card game Guandan and that when presented with very long valid-action lists LLMs strongly prefer the first few listed actions. It also shows that inserting an RL-based action recommender (top-k filtering) and ToM-guided planning raises performance across multiple LLMs and languages.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: An explicitly observed failure mode: action-list overload combined with a front-of-list selection bias in LLM decision behavior in a realistic multi-agent game, and the empirical finding that an RL-based top-k action recommender plus ToM planning mitigates this failure mode.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides concrete, measured evidence of the action-list overload/front-bias in a complex cooperative imperfect-information setting and demonstrates a reproducible mitigation (top-k recommender + ToM) that improves outcomes across model families and language settings.

aggregated_results.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work (RAG, ReAct, Reflexion, ExpeL and related retrieval/reflection methods) established that retrieval or episodic reflection can improve LLM outputs and can help per-task adaptation in text-only settings. Empirical evidence for large, consistent gains specifically in sequential decision-making across both text games and embodied multimodal benchmarks was limited in the literature.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: This paper empirically shows that contextually keyed retrieval of compact episodic traces (task/plan/trajectory windows) substantially improves success rates and rewards across four diverse benchmarks (ALFWorld, WebShop, Franka Kitchen, Meta-World) and across different LLM/VLM backbones.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The concrete cross-benchmark empirical effect—that selective, modality-aware episodic retrieval plus windowed composition into in-context examples yields consistent, large improvements for both text and embodied multimodal agents—is demonstrated and quantified here.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that a zero-shot prompted LLM augmented with external symbolic modules attains high performance on several symbolic text-game tasks and reports task-specific memory-related failure modes (e.g., forgetting navigation routes, limited capacity affecting sorting). It does not formulate a general, named phenomenon linking memory organization to scaling or cross-task generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a general phenomenon: memory augmentation benefits for LLM agents depend critically on memory structure, selective retrieval, and composition (structured relational stores + episodic/reflection), not merely on memory volume or modality.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper contains empirical hints about memory limits and failure modes but does not explicitly identify, name, or systematically characterize the proposed general phenomenon that structured/selective memory (vs. brute-force context) is the decisive factor for scaling and generalization.

aggregated_results.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates an empirical phenomenon: replacing full-text observation histories with Unix-style text deltas (diff history) yields large improvements in sample efficiency and final performance for LM agents in procedurally-generated text environments (strong effect in NetHack, efficiency gains in BabyAI-Text). It also documents environment-dependent behavior (token compression in high-dimensional NetHack, token inflation in BabyAI-Text).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory-level phenomenon asserts a broader, domain-general regularity: robust performance and generalization of LLM agents in complex, partially-observable, long-horizon tasks require not only memory volume but a principled hybrid of structured memory and episodic/reflection memory with selective retrieval/composition.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper establishes that a particular form of structured memory (diff deltas) materially improves agent behavior, but it does not empirically establish the stronger, domain-general phenomenon that a hybrid structured+episodic memory is a fundamental requirement across LLM-agent domains.

aggregated_results.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically shows that assembling multiple frozen, role-aligned policy experts plus a single trainable ("hot") expert with an attention module and a knowledge-graph (KG) state representation yields improved zero-shot and few-shot transfer and greater sample efficiency in their LIGHT-based open-ended text-roleplaying tasks. It also reports failure modes: degraded performance when relevant experts are absent and sensitivity to many irrelevant (distractor) experts. These effects are measured and reported across seeds and experimental conditions.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement asserts a broader phenomenon: that memory augmentation benefits depend more on memory structure, selective access, and composition than on brute memory volume or modality, i.e., structured/selective memory is as critical as memory size or modality for robust agent performance.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides a concrete instance consistent with the theory (structured KG + selective attention over experts helps transfer), but it does not establish the general phenomenon that structure/selectivity universally trumps or equates to memory size/modality across agents, tasks, or long-horizon settings.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that pretrained autoregressive LMs (GPT2-medium) fine-tuned with a small set of demonstrations and augmented via iterated action modeling (IAM) achieve large improvements in ALFWorld; IAM can recover much of the performance of having many more demonstrations. The implementation relies on concatenating goal/observations/actions into a sliding context window (max sequence length 1000 tokens) during training and evaluation.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory claims a distinct empirical phenomenon: robust, scalable performance in complex, partially-observable, long-horizon text games requires a hybrid of structured (relational/graph/attribute) and episodic/reflection memory and selective retrieval/composition — not merely greater memory volume or naive concatenation.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The ALFWorld paper does not identify or name this phenomenon (the necessity of hybrid structured–episodic memory or a systematic failure mode of unstructured concatenation) and provides no experiments or measurements that establish it.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper establishes an empirical phenomenon: storing compact, verbal self-reflections (short natural-language summaries of failed trials) in a small long-term memory yields substantial, consistent performance improvements for LLM agents across decision-making (ALFWorld), multi-hop QA (HotPotQA), and code generation (HumanEval/MBPP/Leetcode) compared to baselines and to na"ive episodic trajectory inclusion.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a broader phenomenon: robust performance and generalization in LLM-based text-game agents fundamentally require a hybrid of structured (graphs/attributes) and episodic/reflection-based memory, with selectivity and retrieval/composition being critical.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The Reflexion paper documents the benefit of distilled verbal episodic memory but does not identify, test, or demonstrate the specific phenomenon that a hybrid structured+episodic memory is necessary; that specific phenomenon is not present in the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically shows that augmenting LLM agents with positive-experience reflection plus a managed memory (dual short-term/long-term buffers that store reflection tuples) improves success rates on the ScienceWorld benchmark relative to baselines (ReAct, Reflexion, CALM), with larger relative gains for smaller models and a performance drop when sampling only from failures. It documents the behavioral regularity that including successful experiences sustains momentum and reduces 'tilt' in medium-difficulty tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory claims a broader phenomenon: robust performance and generalization in LLM-based text-game agents require an integrated hybrid of structured (graph/attribute/summary) and episodic/reflection memory rather than merely more or persistent unstructured memory.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper demonstrates an episodic/managed-memory phenomenon (benefit of positive reflections) but does not identify or empirically demonstrate the specific phenomenon that hybridizing structured relational memory with episodic reflection is necessary for robustness or generalization.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates empirically that structured memory in the form of knowledge graphs improves state modeling and valid-action prediction in partially observable text games, and that predicting graph differences (G_{t+1}-G_t) yields a much smaller, more tractable target than predicting full next-state graphs. It shows consistent performance gains across multiple unseen test games and ablations validating these effects within the text-game domain.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory claims a broader phenomenon: that robust performance and generalization of LLM-based agents in complex, partially observable, long-horizon domains require a principled hybrid of structured (relational) memory and episodic/reflective memory, plus selective retrieval and composition mechanisms.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The necessity of integrating structured relational memory with episodic/reflective memory (and the claim that structure/selectivity/retrieval are as important as mere memory size or modality) is not empirically or conceptually established in the paper and therefore represents a new phenomenon relative to this work.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work on text-based games used learned textual encoders (often trained from scratch), object-centric retrieval, and explicit knowledge-graph state representations; some works used LMs for action generation but not as a frozen/fine-tuned encoder feeding value networks. Empirically, agents trained end-to-end from scratch suffer sample inefficiency and memorization in Jericho benchmarks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: This paper demonstrates that inserting a compact pre-trained transformer (DistilBERT), fine-tuned on out-of-evaluation human game transcripts and used as the encoder for DRRN/TDQN, measurably improves sample efficiency, stability, and final scores across a suite of 14 IF games and attains new SOTA on several games.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The concrete empirical effect — that a single compact pre-trained LM, fine-tuned on general gameplay transcripts and reused (without per-game LM fine-tuning) as the language encoder, can substitute for more complex/structured components and improve RL learning in Jericho games — is not previously established in the cited literature and is presented as a central empirical finding.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work has shown LLMs can serve as commonsense priors for planning (e.g., LLM-as-policy/LLM-MCTS) and that separate memory or structured-state representations can aid text-game agents; vanilla LLM agents and naive MCTS/PUCT struggle on long-horizon, sparse-reward text games with bottlenecks. Empirical benefits of reflective/self-improving LLMs have been demonstrated in interactive settings, but mostly outside of MCTS-integration at scale.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: This paper demonstrates the empirical phenomenon that conditioning an LLM prior inside PUCT on a short in-trial trajectory window plus a bounded set of cross-trial episodic 'reflections' materially improves initial-planning performance across nine Jericho games and helps the search avoid bottleneck failure modes. Ablations show removing cross-trial or in-trial memory substantially degrades scores, indicating the memory modalities are causally related to the observed gains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The specific empirical regularity—that selective, bounded cross-trial reflections combined with short in-trial context change the LLM action prior during MCTS simulations in a way that yields large, consistent initial-planning gains on challenging text-game benchmarks—is not previously documented.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that a pretrained language-model-based action generator (CALM) combined with an RL re-ranker (DRRN) yields higher average normalized scores on a suite of 28 Jericho text games than prior handicap-free baselines, and that generation hyperparameters (top-k) trade off precision vs. recall of admissible and gold actions. It also shows that pretraining on WebText plus fine-tuning on human transcripts materially improves the quality of generated, parseable actions.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory asserts a distinct empirical phenomenon: robust generalization and scaling in LLM-based text-game agents fundamentally require a hybrid memory architecture that integrates structured relational stores (e.g., knowledge/ belief graphs) with episodic/reflection memory and selective retrieval/composition.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper documents the practical benefit of selective candidate generation and the k-dependent precision/recall tradeoff, but it does not identify or empirically demonstrate the novel phenomenon that a principled hybrid of structured relational memory and episodic/reflection memory is a necessary condition for robust scaling/generalization; that necessity is not observed in the paper.

explanatory_mechanistic.what_is_known: One paper (AriGraph) implements a hybrid semantic-graph + episodic-memory architecture with explicit two-stage retrieval (semantic search followed by episodic ranking) as an operational mechanism for improved agent performance in its specific setting. Four additional papers provide explicit but peripheral mechanistic components involving retrieval, reflection, structured memory, or belief inference, though these are presented as engineering choices rather than theoretical principles. Six papers describe or implement mechanisms from which the hybrid architecture is derivable through synthesis.
explanatory_mechanistic.what_introduced: The theory elevates these scattered implementations into a general mechanistic principle: that robust LLM-agent performance across complex, partially observable text games requires the synergistic interaction between structured relational memory (graphs/attributes for compact representations enabling navigation and reasoning) and episodic/reflection memory (for dynamic adaptation and procedural recall), mediated by selective retrieval and composition mechanisms (semantic search, attribute filtering, graph traversal) that balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and reasoning depth. Crucially, the theory claims this synergy is necessary, not merely beneficial, and that neither modality alone suffices for scaling to complex settings.
explanatory_mechanistic.what_novel: While AriGraph demonstrates a concrete instantiation and several papers provide derivable components, the formalization of this mechanism as a necessary, domain-general principle for robust LLM agents represents novel theoretical synthesis. The claim that selective retrieval and composition are critical mediating mechanisms, and that the synergistic causal roles (not additive benefits) of structured and episodic memory are required for generalization and strategic flexibility across diverse text games, is derivable from the collective evidence but is not explicitly stated or established as a recognized theoretical law in any single paper or across the literature.

unification.what_is_known: The literature contains explicit architectural demonstrations of integrated structured+episodic memory systems (AriGraph, Generative Agents) with empirical validation, survey-level taxonomies organizing memory/retrieval designs across systems, and practical pipelines (RAP, MemInsight) combining modalities. Multiple papers show through ablations that these components are complementary and jointly beneficial. The architectural pattern of combining graph-based state, episodic traces, and selective retrieval is well-documented as an effective design choice.
unification.what_introduced: The theory elevates this architectural pattern to a unifying principle: that structured relational memory, episodic/reflection memory, and selective retrieval are not merely complementary techniques but instances of a single fundamental requirement—a necessary (not optional) hybrid memory framework whose principled integration is the underlying explanation for robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility across complex LLM-agent tasks.
unification.what_novel: While the architectural integration is explicitly established and its benefits empirically validated, the elevation to a necessity claim and single-principle unification is not explicitly stated in the literature. Papers frame integration as effective methodology or architecture-specific design rather than as a general law-like requirement. The necessity framing ('neither alone is sufficient'; 'required to balance') and the claim that diverse approaches are instances of one underlying principle can be derived by synthesizing empirical evidence across papers, but this synthesis is not explicitly articulated as a unifying theory in the reviewed literature.

generalization_scope_expansion.what_is_known: A comprehensive survey explicitly documents memory structure (graphs, attributes), selective retrieval (semantic similarity, filtering), and compositional mechanisms as effective design principles across six game genres, with validated special-case caveats (trivial tasks, random environments). Additionally, 14 papers demonstrate these techniques' benefits in specific domains: interactive fiction (GATA, CALM, KG-A2C, Worldformer), embodied/multimodal tasks (RAP), dialogue (MemInsight), role-playing (Thespian, Generative Agents), planning (Reflexion, AriGraph), and cooperative games (Guandan, Sweet&Sour).
generalization_scope_expansion.what_introduced: The theory statement elevates these empirically-validated design principles to a fundamental architectural necessity claim: that memory augmentation benefits depend not just on presence or volume but **crucially** on structure, access patterns, and composition; and that unstructured brute-force or naive concatenation **fails to scale** in complex games. This frames structure/selectivity/composition as **co-equal in criticality** to memory size or modality—a stronger necessity claim than 'effective technique'—and positions it as a domain-general requirement across all nontrivial partially-observable, long-horizon LLM agent settings.
generalization_scope_expansion.what_novel: The elevation from 'effective and broadly-applicable design principle' (established in survey) to 'fundamental architectural necessity that is as critical as memory size itself and whose absence causes scaling failure' represents a scope and strength expansion. While the survey and multiple papers establish that these techniques work well across domains, the specific claim that they are **necessary** (not just beneficial), **co-equal** to memory size, and that naive approaches **fail to scale** is stated more strongly than established literature. This represents an explicit but peripheral claim—the core insight is documented, but the necessity framing and co-equal importance assertion go beyond established statements.

constraint_limitation.what_is_known: The constraint that naive unstructured memory approaches (brute-force context-window concatenation, full-history retention without selection, unfiltered episodic logs) lead to information overload, token inefficiency, noise injection, and failure to scale in complex, long-horizon, or partially observable text games is explicitly established across at least six papers (RAP, AriGraph, MemInsight, Generative Agents, Survey on LLMGAs, Knowledge-enhanced Agents). These papers document concrete failure modes through empirical ablations, quantify performance degradation and token overhead, and propose specific mitigation strategies including selective retrieval, structured memory representations (graphs, summaries), windowing, and embedding-based filtering.
constraint_limitation.what_introduced: The theory statement synthesizes this well-documented limitation into a general principle applicable across LLM-based text game agents: that unstructured retention and naive concatenation are insufficient for robust performance and generalization in complex games, and that selective, structured memory composition (combining retrieval methods like semantic similarity, graph traversal, and attribute filtering with structured representations) is necessary to balance compression, relevance, and reasoning depth. The statement frames this as a fundamental architectural requirement rather than a task-specific optimization.
constraint_limitation.what_novel: The constraint itself is not novel—it is explicitly established, empirically validated, and widely recognized as a known limitation that motivates current architectural choices in the LLM-agent literature. Multiple papers treat this limitation as prior knowledge justifying their design decisions. The theory's contribution lies in synthesizing observations from multiple specific contexts into an explicit general principle with clear scope conditions, but the core constraint would be recognized by domain experts as established knowledge rather than a new discovery.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.what_is_known: Survey papers and comprehensive agent architectures explicitly decompose memory into components (working vs. long-term, semantic vs. episodic, structured vs. unstructured) and discuss retrieval mechanisms (semantic search, graph traversal, filtering) as functional modules within agent architectures. Multiple papers implement structured memory representations (knowledge graphs, belief graphs, attribute stores) coupled with retrieval strategies, treating these as important engineering choices for agent design. This body of work establishes that memory organization and access patterns are recognized considerations in LLM-agent design.
conceptual_reframing_abstraction.what_introduced: The theory reframes memory design by asserting that structure, access patterns, and composition are **as fundamental as** memory capacity or modality—elevating them from implementation details to first-class architectural principles. It explicitly contrasts principled integration of structured and episodic memory with selective retrieval against naive approaches (brute-force concatenation, unstructured logs), positioning 'how memory is organized and accessed' as the **primary** determinant of robust performance rather than a secondary concern. The reframing synthesizes existing components into a unified design principle emphasizing explicit tradeoffs between information compression, retrieval relevance, and reasoning depth.
conceptual_reframing_abstraction.what_novel: While memory decomposition and retrieval mechanisms are explicitly discussed in surveys and implemented across papers (35% treat them as established/peripheral), the specific assertion that structure/selectivity/retrieval are **co-equal in importance** to capacity/modality as a general organizing principle is not consistently established. The majority of papers (52%, 12/23) implement related techniques without advocating this particular hierarchy of design priorities. The conceptual elevation from 'important consideration' to 'as critical as capacity' and the systematic organization of tradeoffs represents a shift in emphasis that is present implicitly across the literature but not formalized as a recognized principle in survey/review papers.

empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_is_known: The empirical pattern that memory structure, selective retrieval mechanisms, and composition strategies significantly impact LLM agent performance is explicitly established in the literature: the Survey paper synthesizes cross-study evidence showing summarization/compression, selective retrieval, and structured representations (graphs/trees) improve long-horizon performance and enable multi-hop reasoning; AriGraph experimentally validates that hybrid structured+episodic memory with selective retrieval yields consistent gains across multiple interactive games and QA benchmarks. Additionally, 4 papers provide explicit peripheral evidence and 12 papers report results from which this pattern is clearly derivable, creating a robust empirical foundation.
empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_introduced: The theory statement synthesizes this established empirical pattern into a normative principle: that structure, selectivity, and retrieval are 'as critical as' memory size or modality, and that unstructured brute-force approaches 'fail to scale.' This elevates documented best practices into a comparative claim of equal or greater importance and frames the pattern as a fundamental architectural requirement rather than one beneficial design choice among many.
empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_novel: The core empirical regularity—that these memory design factors substantially impact performance—is established knowledge, explicitly documented in survey synthesis and systematic experiments. The theory's novel contribution lies in its stronger normative and comparative framing: positioning structure/selectivity/retrieval as equally critical to raw capacity and asserting their necessity as a 'fundamental requirement' across all complex agent settings. This represents a stronger interpretative claim than the 'qualitative best-practice patterns' documented in surveys, though the underlying empirical foundation is well-established.
